1  The English language
The use of English
1.1 English is the world’s most widely used language. A distinction is often
made that depends on how the language is learned: as a native language
(or mother tongue), acquired when the speaker is a young child (generally
|                       |     |                      | acquired at  | some subsequent |     |
| --------------------- | --- | -------------------- | ------------ | --------------- | --- |
| in  the home), or as  |     | a foreign language,  |              |                 |     |
period. Overlapping with this distinction is that between its use as a first
| language,  | the  primary language of the speaker, and  |                           |     | as                | an additional |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------- | --- | ----------------- | ------------- |
| language.  | In  some                                   | countries  (particularly  |     | of course  where  | it  is  the   |
for  internal
| dominant  | native  language),  | English  | is  used  | principally  |     |
| --------- | ------------------- | -------- | --------- | ------------ | --- |
purposes as an intranational language, for speakers to communicate with
other speakers of the same country; in other countries such as Germany
and Japan, it serves chiefly as an international language, the medium of
communication with speakers from other countries.
But in numerous countries such as India, the Philippines, and Nigeria,
where English is for the most part a foreign language too, it nonetheless
has prominent internal functions within these countries in addition to its
international role. Such domestic use of ‘English as a foreign language’ is
often called ‘English as a second language’.
The meanings of ‘grammar’
Syntax and inflections
1.2 We shall be using ‘grammar’ in this book to include both SYNTAX and that
aspect of MORPHOLOGY (the internal structure of words) that deals with
INFLECTIONS (Or ACCIDENCE). The fact that the past tense of buy is bought
{inflection] and the fact that the interrogative form of He bought itis Did he
buy it? [syntax] are therefore both equally within grammar. Our usage
corresponds to one of the common lay uses of the word in the English-
speaking world. A teacher may comment:
John uses good grammar but his spelling is awful.
The comment shows that spelling is excluded from grammar; and if John
| wrote interloper where  |     | the context demanded                        |     | interpreter, the  | teacher |
| ----------------------- | --- | ------------------------------------------- | --- | ----------------- | ------- |
| would say that he had   |     | used the wrong word, not that he had made a |     |                   |         |

the world.

the media. We also frequently need to label features according to variation

in attitude, drawing attention to those that are formal or informal.
The metaphor of the common core points to a distinction that applies to
two other aspects of our description of English grammar. We distinguish
between the central and the marginal also for acceptability and frequency.
| Acceptability is  | a concept that does not apply exclusively to grammar. |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

Elements
2.3 One of the indications that the subject is a clearly identifiable constituent
ofa sentence is, as we have seen, a specific question:
Who went off without paying?

That elderly man (went off without paying).

20 A general framework
The primacy of speech 21
The second variant in this example draws attention to a further
characteristic of the operator: it can be followed by the informal saying that the predication in positive statements is ‘assertive territory’
contraction 7’t (as well as by the full form nov). and that the predication in negative sentences and in questions is
The position immediately after the operator is in fact crucial in forming ‘nonasserlive territory’. While most words can be used equally in assertive
a negation or a question: and nonassertive predications, some determiners, pronouns, and adverbs
(a) Negation is expressed by inserting nof (informally n's) after the have specifically assertive or nonassertive use. Sce further, 10.37.
operator:
NOTE [a] As well as assertive and nonasscrtive forms, there are also some negative forms.
They should have bought a new house. Compare
They should not have bought a new house.
I saw somebody. {assertive pronoun]
(b) Questions are formed by placing the subject of the sentence after the I didn’t see anybody. {nonassertive pronoun]
operator: I] saw nobody, {negative pronoun]
They should have bought a new house. [b] Nonassertive territory is nol confined to negation and question predications, as
Should they have bought a new house?
we shall sce in 10.37 Note [b]; for cxample
If you ever want anything, please ask.
Where the V element in a positive declarative sentence has no operator, a She is more intelligent than anyone IT know.
form of do is introduced as operator in the negative or interrogative
version:
They bought a new house.
They didn’t buy a new house.
Did they buy a new house? The primacy of speech
Where the V element is realized by a form of be, this functions as itself an 2.12 All the material in this book is necessarily expressed in the silence of the
operator:
printed word. But in 2.1 we referred to ‘discourse in speech or writing’,
The sea is very rough. and at no point must we forget that language is normally spoken and
The sea is not very rough. heard. Even what we write and read needs to be accompanied by an
Js the sea very rough? imagined realization in terms of pronunciation and such prosodic features
as stress and intonation. The familiar graphic devices of spaces between
NOTE The verb have can function like be, especially in BrE: words and punctuation marks such as comma, colon, semi-colon, and
She /as the time to spare. period help us to recover from writing how sentences would sound if
She hasn't the time to spare. spoken, but the correspondence between punctuation and prosody is.only
Has she the time to spare? partial. From time to time, we shall need in this book to express examples
with the help of a ‘prosodic transcription’, and we now explain the
But see further 3.14 Notes [a] and [b]. transcription system and the phenomena it represents.
Assertive and nonassertive
Stress, rhythm and intonation
2.11 If we consider the following examples, we see that more can be involved Stress
than what occupies the position after the operator, when we move froma
positive statement as in [1] and [3] to negation or question: 2.13 The relative prominence of a syllable within a word, or ofa word within a
phrase, is indicated by relative stress. In transcription, we mark the
She has finished her thesis already. {1] stressed segment by putting in front ofit a short raised vertical stroke:
She hasn't finished her thesis yet. [2]
in'dignant
The priest gave some money to some of the beggars. (3]
Did the priest give any money to any of the beggars? [4]
in the 'middle
In [2], yet corresponds to the occurrence of already in [1], and in [4] any An exceptionally heavy stress can be shown by a double vertical, and a
twice corresponds to the use of some in [3]. We express these differences by lower level of stress (‘secondary stress’) can be marked by a lowered
vertical stroke. For example:

Pitch prominence at the nucleus is usually associated with pitch change
and the direction of this can be indicated by the use of accents. The
commonest form of pitch change is a fall, as in:

Fullverbs  25
3  Verbs and auxiliaries
{c] Sometimes the main verb (and perhaps other words too) is understood from the
context, so that only auxiliaries are present in the verb phrase:
I can’t tell them, but you can. [ie ‘can tell them’]
Your parents may not have suspected anything, but your sister may have. [ie
“may have suspected something’]
Major verb classes
{d] There are also multi-word verbs, which consist ofa  verb and one or more other
words, eg: turn on, look at, put up with, take place, take advantage of. Cf \6.2ff.
3.1 The term VERB is used in two senses:
1  The verb is one of the elements in clause structure, like the subject and
the object.
| 2  A  verb is                                                            | a member of a word class, like a noun and an adjective. |     |     |     |     |     |     |            |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| The two senses are related in this way: A VERB PHRASE consists of one or |                                                         |     |     |     |     |     |     | Full verbs |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
more verbs (sense 2), eg linked, is making, can believe, might be leaving in
the sentences below; the verb phrase operates as the verb (sense 1) in the
Verb forms
clause, eg:
Regular full verbs, eg: CALL, have four morphological forms: (1) base
They linked hands. form, (2) -s form, (3) -ing participle, (4) -ed form, Irregular full verbs vary
He is making a noise.
I can believe you.
She might be leaving soon. in this respect; for example, the verb SPEAK has five forms, whereas Cut has

If there is only one verb in the verb phrase, it is the MAIN vern. If there is
more than one verb, the final one is the main verb, and the one or more NOTE [a]  Regular verbs are called such because if we know their base form (ie the
verbs that come before it are auxiliaries. For example, /eaving is the main dictionary entry form) we can predict their three other forms (-s, -ing, and -ed) by
verb in this sentence, and might and be are auxiliaries: rule. The vast majority of English verbs are regular, and new words that are coined
or borrowed from other languages adopt the regular pattern.
She might be leaving soon. [b] The primary verb BE (¢f 3.13) has eight forms.
Of the  three classes of verbs, the full verbs can act only as main verbs, the

[b] In both BrE and AmE the general rule is broken by the doubling of -g in
The morphology of irregular full verbs
humbug ~ humbu~ ghugmbiunggged  and of words ending in ¢ (spelled -ck-), eg:
pani~c  panicking~  panicked.  3.9 Irregular  full  verbs  differ  from
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | _   |     |     |     |     |     | regular verbs  |     | in  | that either  | the  past |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | -------------- | --- | --- | ------------ | --------- |
{c] In certain verbs whose base ends in a vowel followed by -s, there is variation inflection, or the -ed participle inflection, or both of these, are irregular.
between -s- and -ss- when the inflection is added:
More precisely the major differences are:
(a)  Irregular verbs either do not have the regular -ed inflection, or else
|     |     | ‘bias  | 'biasing|' biassing  |     |     | ‘biased{' biassed |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | ------ | -------------------- | --- | --- | ----------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
have a variant of that inflection in which the /d/ is devoiced to /t/ (eg:
|     |     | bus  | ‘busing/' bussing  |     |     | bused/bussed |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | ---- | ------------------ | --- | --- | ------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
bu~ rbunrnt,  which occurs alongside the regular burned).
|     |     | ‘focus  | ‘focusing/' focussing  |     |     | ‘focused]' focussed |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | ------- | ---------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
(b)  Irregular verbs typically, but not invariably, have variation in their
Deletion of and addition of -e
base vowel: choose ~ chose ~ chosen, write~  wrote~  written.

Finite verb phrases
He usedn’t (or: used not) to smoke. <BrE>
3.19 A finite verb phrase is a verb phrase in which the first or only word is a
He didn’t use(d} to smoke. ¢BrE and informal AmE)> finite verb (cf 3.3), the rest of the verb phrase (if any) consisting of
The normal interrogative construction is with DO-support, even in BrE: nonfinite verbs. Finite verb phrases can be distinguished as follows:
(a)  Finite verb phrases can
Did he use to drink?  He used to drink, didn't he? occur as  the verb phrase of independent
clauses.
Ought to normally has the to-infinitive, but the fo is optional following (b)  Finite verb phrases have tense contrast, fe the distinction between

At the clause level, changing from active to passive has the following
The indicative was is more common in less formal style.
results:
{a)  The active subject, if retained, becomes the passive AGENT.
NOTE {a] Only were is acceptable in as it were (‘so to speak’); were is usual in if/  were pou.
(b)  The active object becomes the passive subject.
| {b]  | Negation of the present subjunctive does not require an operator. Hence, |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

reconsider in [la] is unambiguously subjunctive: (c)  The preposition Ay is inserted before the agent.
I insist that we not reconsider the Council’s decision,  [la] The butler murdered the detective. [ACTIVE]
The detective was murdered (by the butler). [passive]

48 The semantics of the verb phrase Time, tense, andthe verb 49
opposition is reduced to two tenses: the PRESENT TENSE ane the PAST TENSE, present’, such as Water boils at 100°C and The earth noves round the
which typically refer to present and past time respectively. sun, and more restricted time spans:

(c) The HABITUAL PRESENT PERFECT is used with dynamic verb senses to They Aad moved into the house before the baby was born. [1]
refer to past events that repeatedly occur up to and including the The simple past can often replace the past perfect in such cases, if the time-
present. relationship betwcen the two situations is clear:
T I’ h ve e b m ee a n g a r z e i a n d e i n h g a s o b n e l e y n s c p i u en b c li e s h fi e c d t io e n ve ( r t y l l m n o o n w th ). (since 1975). They moved into the house before the baby was born. [la]
Socrates has influenced many philosophers (till now). The past perfect may also represent the past of the present perfect:
wit U h n a li d k v e e r t b he ia l s s im th p a le t i p n a d s ic t, a t t e h e a p s r p e e s c e i n fi t c p p e o r i f n e t c t o r d o p e e s r io n d ot o n f o ti r m m e a l i l n y t c h o e o p c a c s u t r . She had owned the house since her parents died. [2]

fe] If will (or shall) is combined with the perfect, the resulting future perfect since there is no conception of progression in states of altairs: _
conveys the meaning ‘past in future’:
*T am liking your sister.
By next week, they will have completed their contract. *He was knowing English.
A similar meaning may be conveyed with other modals:
By next weck, they may have completed their contract. [Tt is possible that a W d h o e p n t v d e y r n b a s m t i h c a m t e a a re n in o g r ) d S i : n T a h ri e ly y s m ta ay ti v in e d o i c c a c t u e r a in t y t p h e e o p f r b o e g h re a s v s 1 i o ve u , r w th i e th y

(d)  DYNAMIC PUNCTUAL (with little or no duration)

state, eg: the train is (now) arriving al Platform 4. I had been talking with him only last Monday.
Stance verbs may be used with cither the progressive or the nonprogressive, often I must have been talking with him last M onday
NOTE with little to choose between the variants. But sometimes they seem to be used with
the nonprogressive to express a permanent state and with the progressive to
express a temporary state:
James lives in Copenhagen. [permanent residence] Some means of expressing future time
James is living in Copenhagen. [temporary residence]

If United could have won that game, they might have become  3 She insisted that we Should stay,
“league champions. It’s unfair that so many people should lose their jobs.

Tentativeness or politeness: could, might, would I can’t think why he should have been 50 angry.
4.32 (a)  TENTATIVE PERMISSION (in polite requests): Putative should is more common in BrE than in AmE.

Partitive constructions 71
5 Nouns and determiners classes of nouns, the fourth column showing that some common nouns
can be used as both count and noncount. Thus nouns like cake or brick can
refer either to the substance (noncount) or to units made of the substance
(count). The lines (a)-{e) represent different determiner constraints: Can
the singular noun occur (a) without a determiner? (b) with the definite
article? (c) with the indefinite article? (d) with the partitive some, /sam/?
5.1 Nouns fall into different classes as shown in Fig 5.1. Can the plural noun occur (e) without a determiner?

Partitive constructions
The distinction between concrete (accessible to the senses, observable,
5.2 Both count and noncount nouns can enter constructions denoting part of
measurable, etc) and abstract (typically nonobservable and nonmeasur-
a whole. Such partitive expressions may relate to (a) quantity or (b)
able) is important semantically. Of more relevance to grammar, however,
quality, and in either case the partition may be singular or plural. It thus
is the distinction between proper and common nouns. Since the former
affords a means of imposing number on noncount nouns, since the
have unique reference (cf'5.25/7), determiner and number contrast cannot
partition is generally expressed by a count noun of partitive meaning
occur: *the Indonesia, *some Chicagos. Contrast with common nouns the
(such as piece or sort, which can be singular or plural), followed by an
butter, some difficulties. But among nouns, we have the further distinction
of-phrase.
between count (also called ‘countable’) and noncount (also called ‘mass’)
(a) QUANTITY PARTITION
nouns. Like the distinction between proper and common, we have here a
difference that has both semantic and grammatical significance, since (1) Of noncount nouns; eg:
count and noncount nouns permit a different range of determiners. In a piece of cake two pieces of cake

These partitives (as also the informal dit) can be used very generally,
but with some nouns specific partitives occur; eg:
Table 5.1
a blade of grass
PROPER COMMON some specks of dust
two slices of meat/bread/cake
COUNT NONCOUNT COUNT or NONCOUNT
(a) Freda *book music brick (ii) Of plural count nouns; here we tend to have partitives relating to
(b) *the Freda the book the music the brick specific sets of nouns; eg:
(c) *a Freda a book *a music a brick
(d) *some Freda *some book some music some brick

78 Nouns and determiners
The articles in specific reference 79
Do you see the bird sitting on the lower branch?
He has a fracture of the collarbone.
Oh dear! The stain hasn’t come out of the carpet.
The child has a pain in the chest.
In such cases, the identity of the particular bird, branch, stain, and carpet
In medical usage, the can replace a possessive without the body part or
is obvious because they are physically present and visible. But the
function being in a prepositional phrase; thus (doctor to patient):
reference might be obvious because the situational reference was in the
minds of speaker and listener: How is the chest now? Has the breathing been affected?
When the policeman had gone, | remembered that [hadn’t told him
about tae damaged window-panc. The use of the may be determined by Jogical and grammatical factors. The
uniqueness ofa referent may be recognized not by general knowledge of
Secondly, we have the LARGER situation, where identification of the the world but be logically imposed by meaning. Nouns premodified by
reference depends on assumptions about general knowledge more than on superlatives, ordinals and similar restrictive items such as sole will thus be
the specific experience of the particular speaker and listener: made logically unique:
I do most of my travelling by overnight train, and of course in the When is the next flight?
dark one has no idea of what the countryside looks like. She was the sole survivor,
So also with reference to the Pope, the President, the government, the They judged him to be she most original painter,
Equator, the stars, and as we see in these examples, the shared assumption Grammatical determination is of two kinds:
of uniqueness in reference is often matched by use of an initial capital in (a) Anaphoric reference, where the indicates identity of reference with
writing. Cf 5.2577 ' that established earlier in the discourse:
NOTE The same phrase may involve the with immediate or larger situational reference: Fred bought a radio and a video-recorder, but he returned the radio,
Would the children like to go out and play? Here the anaphora is ‘direct’: but anaphoric reference may be
When we design schools, do we ask ourselves what the children would like? ‘indirect’, requiring some support from general knowledge:
As with the latter example, larger situational reference often overlaps with generic When she tried to open her front door, she couldn’t get the key into
use: ¢f 5.227, the lock.
5.13 Special cases of the larger situation occur with the use of the for SPORADIC Here the two definite articles are correctly interpreted as grammati-
reference and for reference to the Bopy. In sporadic reference, we promote cally anaphoric only because we know that a front door has a lock,
to institutional status a phenomenon of common experience. Thus in and openinonge involves using a key.
contrast to the particular newspaper that a particular individual buys, or (b) Cataphoric reference, where the indicates that the identity of the
the particular theatre that stands in a particular street, we may use the reference will be established by what follows:
paper or the theatre more broadly: Tam trying to find the book that I wanted to show you.
You'll probably see it in the paper tomorrow. Here, the is only justified by the addressee knowing that the speaker
I like to go to the theatre about once a month. had planned to show him or her a book. Similarly, in
Cf also: How did you get the (= ‘that’) mud on your coat?
She’s not on the telephone yet, though she may have one installed there is the presumption that the addressee knows there is mud on the
soon. coat. Contrast:
I won’t come by car; ll take rhe train.
Everyone would sleep better with the windows open. Do y you know that y youh ave { m *th u e d mud } on your coat??
With reference to parts of the body, the is often used in prepositional
phrases instead of a possessive such as my or her: The indefinite article
I grabbed him by the arm. 5.15 In contrast to the, an indefinite article is used when a reference cannot be
She banged herself on the forehead. regarded as pniquely identifiable from the shared knowledge of speaker

The articles in specific reference 83

apposition (17.27): (c) Time expressions: zero is common, especially after the prepositions at,
Prime Minister Gandhi~ the Prime Minister, Mr Rajiv Gandhi by, after, before:

status for an item: by morning/evening (‘when morning/evening came’)
Council will consider this in due course~ The Council will...
by day/night (‘during’)
after dark/nightfall
{c] Articles are usually omitted in headlines (‘Crew deserts ship in harbour’) and on before dawn/dusk
oficial forms (‘Please state reason for application and give names of two
supporters’). Cfalso ‘(They worked) day and night’, ‘It’s almost dawn’, ‘ll be
traveling all night/week/month’,

with implication of definite rather than indefinite meaning. This is beautiful’, ‘I'll rest during the evening’, ‘Can you stay for the night: 2
especially so with idiomatically institutionalized expressions relating to With in, seasons may also have zero, unless a particular one

i

prison Don’t stop near the prison.
be at, go home This was the home of a financier. What time do you normally have break fast/lunch/supper?

sea The sea looks calm.
go to { college She drove to the college. (e) dliness: zero is normal, especially where the illness bears a technica’

Frequently there is a distinction in meaning between zero and the;
thus on stage will usually refer to a play or participant in current
She has anaemia/cancer/diabetes/influenza/pneumonia/toothache
theatrical production, while on the stage refers to literal physical But the is also used for afflictions less technically designated:
location or is an idiom denoting the acting profession: ‘She was a
teacher but now she’s on the stage.’ Again, there are distinctions in
She had (the) flu/hiccups/measles/mumps.
meaning between AmE and BrE; thus in school would be used in AME Some conditions call for the indefinite article:

Whether names have articles (as in 5.28/) or not, they operate without a 5.32 These are used without article and comprise a wide range of desi gnations:
5.30
determiner contrast,  and  while  it  is  normal  for names  to  reflect  the (a)  extraterrestrial: Jupiter, Mars (but the moon, the sun);
uniqueness  of their referents by having no  article,  it must be clearly (b)  continents: Asia, (South ) America;
understood that ‘No article’ does not mean ‘Zero article’ (cf 5.1 Note). (c)  countries, provinces, ete: (Great) Britain, Canada, Ontario, (County)
There are two major classes of names to consider: names ofp ersons and
Kerry (but the United Kingdom, (the) Sudan);
names ofp laces. On smaller classes, such as the names of months, see 5.25. (d)  lakes: Lake M.i chigan, Loch Ness, Ullswater;
{e)  mountains: ( Mount) Everest, Snowdon:

94 Nouns and determiners Number 95
for example: /d~e /dier/z/ /be~ /bded/z/ Irregular plural formation
— add /s/ if the singular ends with a voiceless consonant other than a Voicing
sibilant 5.37 While in spelling the pair hou~ hsousees is regular, in pronunciation it is
for example: /kat/~ /kats/ not, the final voiceless fricative consonant of the singular becoming voiced
in the plural: /haus/~ /hauziz/. Several singulars ending in /f/ and /0/
(b) In SPELLING: undergo voicing in this way, the former reflected in spelling, the latter not:
With the vast majority of nouns, we simply add -s to the singular; for
example: knife ~ knives /na~ /rnafrv/z/
mouth~mouths /mau@/~/maudz/
horse ~ horses, prize ~ prizes
Like knife are calf, half, leaf, life, loaf, self, shelf, thief, wife, wolf, and a few
But, quite apart from the nouns that are fundamentally irregular in others. With some nouns, such as handkerchief, hoof, and scarf, the plural
respect of number (5.37/f), the -s rule requires amplification and may involve voicing or be regular (-/fs/); with others, such as belief, cliff,
modification for many nouns: proof, the plural is always regular.
(i) Ifthe singular ends with a sibilant (see (a) above) that is not already Like mouth are bath, oath, path, sheath, truth, wreath, youth, though in
followed by -e, the plural ending is -es; for example: b~o boxxes, most cases the plural can equally be regular (-/0s/). In other cases, only the
bush ~ bushes, switch ~ switches; of language ~ languages. regular plural is found, as with cloth, death, faith, moth, and where there is
(ii) Ifthe singular ends with -y, this is replaced by /and the plural ending a consonant preceding the fricative this is always so (as with birth, length,
is then -ies; for example: spy ~ spies, poppy ~ poppies, soliloquy ~ sol- etc),
iloquies. But -y remains, and the plural ending is -ys, if the singular
ends with a letter having vowel-value as in -ay, -ey, -oy (thus days, Vowel change
ospreys, boys), or if the item is a proper noun (the two Germanys; cf $.38 In asmall number of nouns, there is a change of vowel sound and spelling
5.26). (‘mutation plurals’) without an ending:
(iit) If the singular ends with -o, the plural is usually regular (as with
foo~t feet goose ~ geese
studios, kangaroos, pianos), but with some nouns the plural ending is
louse ~ lice man~ men
-es (as with echoes, embargoes, heroes, potatoes, tomatoes, torpedoes,
mouse ~ mice tooth ~ teeth
vetoes), and in a few cases there is variation, as with buffalo(e)s,
woman /’wumen/~ women /‘wimin/
cargo(e)s, halo(e)s, motto(e)s, volcano(e)s.

5.44  (a)  Binary nouns are those that refer to entities which comprise or are
perceived as comprising two parts: tools and instruments such as
binoculars, forceps, Scissors;
articles of dress such as Jeans, pants,

Genitive meanings
The /kau/ was grazing.
The /kauz/ were grazing.
One /kauz/ tail was waving.
All the /kauz/ tails were waving. The meanings  expressed  by  the  genitive  can  conveniently  be  shown

Socrates’ /-ti:z/ doctrines
Moses’ /-ziz/ laws The prisoner's release.  (. .  .) released the prisoner.
But Dickens’ novels /dikinz/ or /dikinzatz/ Cf The release of the prisoner.
Note the zero genitive also in some expressions such as for goodness’ sake.
(f)  Genitive of origin:

\Uo  Nouns and determiners
Case  107
Where did you find these children’s clothes?
One of George's sisters is coming to stay with us.
(Either ‘These children had lost their clothes’; genitive as determiner. Or A sister of George's is coming to stay with us.
‘These clothes were obviously made for children’: genitive as modifier.)
It is the latter that is called the ‘post-genitive’ (or ‘double-genitive’).
The independent genitive
5.54 It is common to ellipt the noun following the genitive if the reference is Bibliographical note
contextually clear. For example:
On noun classes, see Algeo (1973); Allerton (1987); Seppanen (1974).
On reference and determiners, see Auwera (1980);
Jennifer's is the only face I recognize here. Burton-Roberts (1977);
|     |     |     |     |     |     | Declerck (1986);  |     | Hawkins (1978);  |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ----------------- | --- | ---------------- | --- | --- | --- |
(ie Jennifer’s face) Hewson  (1972);  Kaluza  (1981);  Kramsky
|     |      |               |                           |                                 |     | (1  | 9 7 2 ) ;   P e | r lm u t              |                    |                                |                                               |
| --- | ---- | ------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --- | --- | --------------- | --------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------- |
|     | He   | h             |                           |                                 |     |     |                 | t e r   ( 1           | 9 7 0 ) ;   T a k  | a m i   (1 9 8 5 ) .           |                                               |
|     |      | a s   a   d   | e v o t i o n   t o   w   | o r k  l i ke   A is  father’s. |     |     | O n   n u m     | b                     |                    |                                |                                               |
|     |      |               |                           |                                 |     |     |                 | e r ,  s c e   H      | ir t l e   ( 1 9 8 | 2 ) ;   J u u l   ( 1 9 7 5 ); |   L e h r e r  ( 1 9 86); Sorensen (1985). On |
|     | ( ie |   h i s   f a | th e r ’ s   d e v o t io | n   to   w o r k )              |     | g e | n d e r         |                       |                    |                                |                                               |
|     |      |               |                           |                                 |     |     | a n d           |   c a s e ,   s e c   | D a h l   ( 1 9    | 7 1 ) ;   J a h r  S e r h     | c i m   ( 1 9 8 0 ) .                         |
By contrast, with the oef-construction, that or those usually replaces the
corresponding item:
The wines of France are more expensive than those of California.
(ie the wines of California)
A special case of the independent genitive occurs when the unexpressed
item refers to homes or businesses:
When I arrived at Fred’s, I found I'd come on the wrong day.
My butcher's stays open late on Fridays.
She wouldn’t miss St Martin’s on Easter morning,
| In  | most such instances  |     | of the  | ‘local genitive’,  |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | -------------------- | --- | ------- | ------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
one could not specify
uniquely the unexpressed item, and in many cases it would sound artificial
if one attempted a fuller phrase, often because what is meant is more
abstract and general than any specific noun would convey:
I hate going to the dentist’s (? surgery, ? place, ? establishment).
NOTE With the names of major firms, what begins as a local genitive develops into a
plural, often so spelled and observing plural concord:
Harrod’s is a vast store.
|     | Harrods are having a  |     | sale. |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --------------------- | --- | ----- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
A further development is to drop the ending and to treat the item as a collective (of
5.46).
The ‘post-genitive’
5.55 Since in its determiner role, the genitive must be definite (5.52), we can be
in some difficulty with a sentence like
George's sister is coming to stay with us.
Ifit needs to be understood that George has more than one sister, this can
| be  | expressed  | in  | one  of  two  | ways,  each  involving  | a  partitive  |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | ---------- | --- | ------------- | ----------------------- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
of-
construction:

Which is his wife? The woman nearest the door. In consequence, this/these tend to be associated with cataphoric reference
(5.14), that/those with anaphoric reference:
Demonstrative pronouns Watch carefully and I'll show you: this is how it’s done.
6.19  The demonstratives have the same formal range and semantic contrast So now you know: fhat’s how it’s done.
both as pronouns and as determiners (5.5), this/these suggesting relative This is an announcement: will Mrs Peterson please go to the enquiry

But while all can be used as determiners irrespective of the gender of the
noun head, as pronouns the reference must be to nouns of nonpersonal
(and usually inanimate) gender:

When used pronominally, some and any usually have clear contextual
reference to a noun phrase. Both occur more freely as determiners:
(a) I would love some nuts and some wine, please. The partitives include quantifiers, which may (a) increase or (b) decrease
(b) If you haven’t any nuts, Pil not have any wine, thank you. the  implications  Or {  |S OME 4  thus  behseide  There  are  sorie

The examples above illustrate the use of these items with personal,
nonpersonal, count, and noncount reference, But it should be further
(a) There are many who would disagree.
noted that with any the number distinction is typically blurred:
(b) There are a few who would disagree.
The woman said she’d seen an animal running for cover, but her
Analogously with noncount reference:
companion said that he hadn’t seen any animal(s) at all.

With noncount partition:
By contrast, other does not enter into of-partitives, but in its plural form is
otherwise common in pronoun usage:
all of You should treat others as you would like to be treated yourself.
some of
a great deal of [a] In association with each and one, other and another function as reciprocal
| much of |     | pronouns. For example: |     |     |

more of

least of

any of
out below. As ordinals, items are usually preceded by the, as fractions by aor one:
| none of  | J   |     |     |     |

She played much less of Beethoven’s music than we had hoped.
3 three  3rd third

| 14 fourteen   | 14th fourteenth  |                                  |     |     |     |     |
| ------------- | ---------------- | -------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 15 fifteen    | [5th fifteenth   |                                  |     |     |     |     |

71 Four features are commonly considered to be characteristic of adjectives:
18 eighteen  18th eighteenth (a)  They can freely occur in ATTRIBUTIVE function, ie they can premodify
| 19 nineteen  | 19th nineteenth |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------ | --------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
a noun, appearing between the determiner (including zero article) and
| 20 twenty  | 20th twentieth |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------- | -------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
the head ofa  noun phrase:
| 21 twenty-one  | 21st twenty-first |     |     |     |     |     |
| -------------- | ----------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
22 twenty-two  22nd twenty-second an ug/y painting, the round table, dirty linen
| 23 twenty-three  | 23rd twenty-third |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------------- | ----------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
24 twenty-four (etc)  24th twenty-fourth (etc) (b)  They can freely occur in PREDICATIVE function, ie they can function as
30 thirty  30th thirtieth subject complement, as in [1], or as object complement, as in [2], eg:
| 40 forty  | 40th fortieth |     |     |     |     |     |
| --------- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
The painting is ugly.
| 50 fifty  | 50th fiftieth |     |     |     |     | []  |
| --------- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
He thought the painting ug/y.
| 60 sixty    | 60th sixtieth   |     |     |     |     | [2] |
| ----------- | --------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 70 seventy  | 70th seventicth |     |     |     |     |     |
(c)  They can be premodified by the intensifier very, eg:
| 80 cighty          | 80th eighticth  |                              |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------ | --------------- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 90 ninety          | 90th ninetieth  | The children are very happy. |     |     |     |     |
| 100 a/one hundred  | 100th hundredth |                              |     |     |     |     |
120 a/one hundred and twenty 120th hundred and twentieth (d)  They can take COMPARATIVE and SUPERLATIVE forms. The comparison
may be by means of inflections (-er and -est), as in [3-4], or by the
| 1,000 a/one thousand | [,000th thousandth |     |     |     |     |     |
| -------------------- | ------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
addition of the premodifiers more and most (‘periphrastic compari-
| 1,500 a/one thousand five hundred | 1,500th thousand five hundredth |                     |     |     |     |     |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000 two thousand (etc)          | 2,000th two thousandth (ete)    | son’), as in [5-6]: |     |     |     |     |
| 100,000 a/one hundred thousand    | 100,000th hundred thousandth    |                     |     |     |     |     |
The children are happier now.
| 1,000,000 a/one million | 1,000,000th millionth |     |     |     |     | (3] |
| ----------------------- | --------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
They are the happiest people I know.
| 1,000,000,000 a/one billion | 1,000,000,000th billionth |                                       |     |     |     | (4] |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|                             |                           | These students are more intelligent.  |     |     |     | [5] |
Bibliographical note They are the most beautiful paintings I have ever seen.
[6]
On pronouns in general, see Bolinger (1979); Jackendoff (1968); Stevenson and Not all words that are traditionally regarded as adjectives possess all
these  four
Vitkovitch (1986). features.  The  last  two  features  generally  coincide  for  a
On  central  pronouns,  see  Helke  (1979);  Jacobsson  (1968);  Saha  (1987); particular word  and  depend  on  a  semantic  feature,  gradability. The
Seppanen (1980); Thavenius (1983); on reciprocal pronouns, see Kjellmer (1982); adjective atomtic in atomic scientist, for example, is not gradable and we
on indefinite pronouns, see Sahlin (1979); on numerals, see Hurford (1975). therefore do not find
|     |     |     | *very atomic or *more atomic.  |     |     | Gradability cuts |
| --- | --- | --- | ------------------------------ | --- | --- | ---------------- |
across word classes. Many adverbs are gradable, and since they also take
|     |     | premodification  | by  very  | and  comparison,  | these  two  | features  |
| --- | --- | ---------------- | --------- | ----------------- | ----------- | --------- |
do  not
distinguish adjectives from adverbs.
|     |     | The  ability  | to        |                |                    |              |
| --- | --- | ------------- | --------- | -------------- | ------------------ | ------------ |
|     |     |               | function  | attributively  | and  the  ability  | to  function |
predicatively are central features of adjectives. Adjectives like happy and
infinite, which have both these features, are therefore CENTRAL adjectives.
Those like wrter that can be only attributive and those like afraid that can
be only predicative are PERIPHERAL adjectives.

the city council ~ the councfoirl  the city 75 There are many adjectives that have the same sullixes as participlesi n -ing

PARTICIPIAL ADJECTIVES:
Such a correspondence is not available for attributive adjectives:
|     |     |     | PREDICATIVE USE |     |     | ATTRIBUTIVE USE |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --------------- | --- | --- | --------------- | --- |
a long poem a thick wall His views were very surprising. ~ his surprising views
|                               |     |     | The man seemed very offended. |     |     | ~ the offended man |     |
| ----------------------------- | --- | --- | ----------------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------ | --- |
| the urban council hot weather |     |     |                               |     |     |                    |     |
They include forms in -ed that have no corresponding verbs:
Some items can be both adjectives and nouns. For example, criminal is
an adjective in that itcan be used both attributively (a criminal attack ) and
|                                               |           |                  | The results were unexpected.      |     |     | ~ the wiexpected results  |     |
| --------------------------------------------- | --------- | ---------------- | --------------------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------------- | --- |
| predicatively (The attack seemed criminal to  | us),  On  | the  other hand, |                                   |     |     |                           |     |
|                                               |           |                  | Her children must be downhearted. |     |     | ~her downhearted children |     |
criminal has been converted into a noun in The criminal pleaded guilty and All his friends are talented.
~his talented friends

~his diseased lung

intellectual interests She considers herself an intellectual. [a] In some cases the -ed participle is not interpreted as passive. The passive

Some nouns can also be used within the subject complement after seem Cesp BrE): the faded curtains [‘the curtains which have faded’ ]
the retired manager [‘the manager who has retired’]
He seems af ool. [= foolish]
Your remark seems (complete) nonsense to me. [= nonsensical] Predicative use occurs only with some of these participial adjectives:
My stay there seemed sheer bliss. [= blissful]
The curtains are faded [‘The curtains have faded’]
His friend seems very much an Englishman. [= very English] Her father is now retired,

A postpositive adjective can usually be regarded as a reduced relative postposition is not normally allowed:
clause:

something that is useful  [3a] *The soldiers (rather) timid approached their officer.
However, if the noun phrase is generic and indefinite, we can postpone
Compound  indefinite pronouns and adverbs ending in -body, -one,
-thing, -where can be modified only postpositively: coordinated adjectives, or adjectives with some clause element added,
though such constructions are formal and rather infrequent:

Unlike types (a) and (b), type (c) adjectives functioning as noun-phrase performance of an action.
heads take singular concord: NOTE The implicd subject of the clause can be the whole of the superordinate clause:
They ventured into the unknown, which was...
Strange, it was she who initiated divorce proceedings.

example, the supernatural, the exotic, the unreal are more likely to occur Here too it is possible to substitute an adverb for the adjective with little or no

The very best is yet to come. 7 Contingent verbless clauses
He went from the extremely sublime to the extremely ridiculous. TAS One type ofv erbless clause, which is often introduced by a subordinator,
{b] There are some set expressions in which an adjective with abstract reference is expresses the circumstance or condition under which what is said in the
superordinate clause applies:
the complement of a preposition:
(Whether) right or wrong, he always comes off worst in an argument
He left for good. He enjoyed it to the full.
because ofh is inability to speak cogently.
in public/private/secret from bad to worse
in short out of the ordinary When fit, the Labrador is an excellent retriever.
on the sly in conunon If wet, these shoes should never be placed too close to the heat.
_ Verbless clauses . The contingent clause can also refer to the object of the superordinate
Adjectives can function as the sole realization ofa verbless clause (cf 14.6, clause, in which case it usually appears in final position:
7.14
15.34f) or as the head of an adjective phrase realizing the clause: You must eat it when fresh.
The man, quietly assertive, spoke to the assembled workers.
Unhappy with the result, she returned to work, NOTE The clause can also refer to the whole of the superordinate clause (which would be
Glad to accept, the boy nodded his agreement. realized in the subordinate clause by the pro-form if), In such cases the
Anxious for a quick decision, the chairman called for a vote.
subordinator cannot be omitted:
Long and untidy, his hair played in the breeze. When (it is) necessary, he can be taken to the doctor.
You must come as soon as (it is) possible.
The clause is mobile:
The chairman called for a vote, anxious for a quick decision.
Exclamatory adjective clauses
7.16 Adjectives can be exclamations, with or without an initial wh-element:
Its implied subject is usually the subject of the sentence (‘The chairman is
anxious for a vote’). However, if the clause contains additional clause Excellent! (How) wonderful! (How) good of you!
constituents, it can be related to a noun phrase other than the subject: Such clauses need not be dependent on any previous linguistic context, but
She glanced with disgust at the cat, now quiet in her daughter’s lap. may be a comment on some object or activity in the situational context.
Sometimes the adjective phrase can be replaced by an adverb phrase
Syntactic subclassification of adjectives
with little change of meaning:
Attributive only
Rather nervous, the man opened the letter.
Rather nervously, the man opened the letter. 717 In general, adjectives that are restricted to attributive position, or that

Stative/dynamic
a criminal lawyer [‘a lawyer specializing in cases of crime’)
a polar bear [‘a bear living near the pole’] 7.23 Adjectives are characteristically stative. Many adjectives, however, can be
a medical school ['a school for students of medicine’] seen as dynamic. In particular, most adjectives that are susceptible to
musical comedy [‘a comedy accompanied by music’} subjective  measurement  are  capable  of being  dynamic.  Stative  and
dynamic adjectives differ syntactically in a number of ways, For example,
a tidal wave [‘a wave produced by the tide’]

. Reaction signal and initiator She wisely didn’t attempt to apologize.
729 Certain other items must be positioned initially. They are important
because of their high frequency in spoken English. Some are restricted to bet C w O e N e J n U N tw CT o S li ( n c g f u 8 is .4 ti 3 c ) u e n x i p ts re , s e s g : the speaker’s assessment of the relation
the spoken language. These can be assigned to two small classes:
(i) ‘reaction signals’, eg: no, yes, yeah, yep, mt, hm, milun She has bought a big house, so she must have a lot of money.
(ii) ‘initiators’, eg: well, oh, ah; oh well, well then, why Cesp AME) We have complained several times about the noise, and yer he does
nothing about it.
Adverb and preposition All our friends are going to Paris this summer. We, however, are
7.30 There are various combinations of verbs plus particles (cf 16.3//). Since a going to London.
preposition is normally followed by its complement, the particle is an If they open all the windows, then I’m leaving.
adverb if the verb is intransitive: I didn’t invite her. She wouldn't have come, anyway.
The plane has taken off.
Adverb as modifier
When a noun phrase follows the particle, the latter may still be an adverb.
Modifier of adjective
The particle in the phrasal verb in [1] is an adverb because it can be
transposed to follow the verb, as in [1a]: _ 7.32 An adverb may premodify an adjective. Most commonly the adverb is an
intensifier or emphasizer (ef 8.36/):
They turned on the light. [1]
They turned the light on. [la] extremely dangerous really beautiful
deeply concerned very good
In contrast, /o in [2] is a preposition, part of the prepositional verb fake to, perfectly reasonable just impossible
because its position is fixed, as we see from [2a]:
Some premodifiers are related to adverbs that express such notions as
They took to us. [2] manner and means but also have some intensifying effect:
*They took us fo. {2a}
easily debatable quietly assertive
openly hostile readily available
Syntactic functions of adverbs
Some premodifiers express ‘viewpoint’ (cf 8.33):
Adverb as adverbial
731 ADJUNCTS and SUBJUNCTS are relatively integrated within the structure of politically expedient (‘expedient from a political point of view’)
the clause (cf 8.13, 8.32). Examples of adjuncts: theoretically sound technically possible
Slowly they walked back home. NOTE — Enough may postmodify an adjective: ofd enough. Like indeed, it may also postmodify an
He spoke to me about it briefly. adverb, though indeed tends to go with a premodifying very: carefully enough, very easily
indeed.

|     |     |     | m o r e  c | l ever |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

Jane is cleverer than her sister.
m o s t  c l e ver
Jane is cleverer than all the other students in the class.
(b) Comparison to the same degree is expressed by as (or sometimes
The superlative is generally used when more than two are involved:
SO)... as:
Jane is the cleverest of the three sisters.
Anna is as tall as Bill.
Jane is the cleverest of all the students in the class.
Anna is not e } tall as John. With the superlative, Jane is included in the group and compared with the
others.

Adverbs that are identical in form with adjectives take inflections if the
adjectives do so: fast, hard, late, long, quick. They follow the same spelling
and phonological rules as for adjectives, eg: early ~ earlier ~ earliest:
The unmarked term in measure expressions
7.45 We use the adjective old in measure expressions (x years old) when we
refer to a person’s age, regardless of the age:
Mr Jespersen is 75 years old.
His granddaughter is two years old.
In the scale of measurement, o/d indicates the upper range (He is ofd) but it
is also the unmarked term for the whole range, so that She is tvo years old
is equivalent to Her age is two years. The measure adjectives used in this
way are the following, with the marked term in parentheses:
| deep (shallow)  | high (low)    | long (short)  old (young)  | tall (short) |     |     |     |     |     |
| --------------- | ------------- | -------------------------- | ------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| thick (thin)    | wide (narrow) |                            |              |     |     |     |     |     |
These unmarked terms are also used in how-questions and, again, they
do not assume the upper range. How old is shei?s  equivalent to What is her
age? Other adjectives are also used in the same way in fow-questions, eg:

174 The semantics and grammar of adverbials
Grammatical functions 175
NOTE Placing of a time adjunct is acutely affected if there is a decided orientation {e] All process adjuncts are normally placed at E, though manner adjuncts can be
towards either the subject or the object; ef 8.15 Note. at M. Cooccurrence of process adjuncts is by no means unusual; for example:
Process adjuncts She was accidentally [manner] struck with a racket {instrument] by her
partner [agent].
Manner adjuncts
8.27 Manner adjuncts are chiefly realized by adverb phrases, by /ike-phrases, Adjuncts of respect
as-clauses, and by prepositional or noun phrases involving such nouns as 8.29 A wide range of realization is available for adjuncts that express the
way and manner; for example: respect in which the truth value of a sentence is being claimed. For
example:
She looked at him coldly. legally.
He walks like his father. on legal issues,
Please don’t speak in that rude way. A neighbour is advising me sof ar as legal matters are concerned.
They cook (in) the) French style. in respect to law,
I wish I could write as you do. front a legal standpoint.
Manner adjuncts are almost always at £, but although thus associated Respect is a relationship often expressed by subjuncts or disjuncts (8.33,
with the greatest rhetorical weight in a clause, there is no simple 8.41), but when adjuncts are involved they are usually predicational and
interrogative device for eliciting them. are placed at £.
Adjuncts of means, instrument, and agency Adjunets of contingency
8.28 There are close semantic similarities between means, instrument, and 8.30 Contingency relations are commonly expressed by disjuncts (8.41), but
agent, and there is considerable overlap in realization. The means and the adjuncts are often used for reason and its correlate purpose, both of which
agent are often expressed with by-phrases, but the latter is grammatically can be elicited by the same question forms, Why ...? What.. _for? as in:
distinct in correlating with the passive (and hence corresponding to a
transitive clause); for example: He di«d4 +i, t { fbecauSse e hHee wwas as an a g ng r ry y . . | [reason
to relieve his anger. [purpose]
He was killed by a terrorist. (~ A terrorist killed him.)
As well as by finite clauses, reason adjuncts are realized by prepositional
A means adjunct, on the other hand, can easily occur in a transitive clause: phrases and nonfinite clauses; for example:
She influenced me by her example. She made the sacrificfoer her son.
There were many deaths from malnutrition.
Instrument adjuncts differ from both means and agent adjuncts in
generally being realized by with-phrases: With him being so angry, 1 didn’t tell him the worst part.
Purpose adjuncts are realized by nonfinite (infinitive) clauses:
He was killed with a hunting knife.
But means and instrument adjuncts can share realization with adverbs; The driver slowed down
(in order)}
to avoid an accident.
(se as)
thus means in:
In formal style, finite clauses can occur:
They decided to treat the patient surgically. (=‘by means of
surgery’) Inoculation must be carried out /est the disease spread.
He died (in order) that others might live.
Similarly, instrument in:
She examined the specimen microscopically. (where the adverb is
But some forms of finite clause are used more generally:
here intended to mean ‘with a microscope’) Turn the gas off in case there's an explosion.
We'd better leave now so we can get home before dark.
NOTE {a] If in the last example, microscopically meant ‘in microscopic detail’, the adjunct
would be one of manner (8.27) and could be gradable, ‘(quite) microscopically’. When the concessive relation is expressed by an adjunct, this takes the
[b] Means and instrument adjuncts can be clicited by Aow-questions: ‘How are you form of a prepositional phrase:
travelling to Hamburg?’ ‘By air/By Lufthansa’. She gave the lecture despite her illness.

176 The semantics and grammar of adverbials
Subjuncts 177
Contingency adjuncts are usually sentential and although normally at Architecturally, the plans represent a magnificent conception.
£ they are commonly (especially those of purpose and concession) placed
at J.
But there can be other forms of realization:
Relative positions of adjuncts From a personal viewpoint, he is likely to do well in this post.
Looked at politically, the proposal seems dangerous.
8.31 Looking now at the whole range of adjunct types (8.16-8.30), we can
consider some general principles oft heir relative ordering in cooccurence: Especially in AmE, we find adverbs in -iise:
(a) the order, especially of sentence adjuncts, can be dictated by such
Weatherwise, the outlook is dismal,
considerations as what can be taken for granted and what needs to
have most impact (cf 18.4/on/ information processing); Courtesy subjuncts
(b) shorter adjuncts tend to precede longer ones. 8.34 A small number of adverbs in -ly, along with please, serve to convey a
But subject to (a) and (b), where adjuncts cluster at E, they will tend to formulaic tone of politeness to a sentence. They normally occur at M:
occur in the following sequence:
You are cordially invited to take your places.
respect — process - space — time — contingency He asked ifI would please read his manuscript.
It is less usual to find more than one adjunct at J, but any such cluster Courtesy subjuncts obviously involve the semantic calegory manner but
would tend to follow a converse order; eg: space (or process) -- time. are quite distinct from manner adjuncts. Contrast:

Content disjuncts
Although not restricted as to position (and while some, as we shall see, are
often at M), most disjuncts appear at /. 8.42 Comment on the content of an utterance may be of two kinds:
(a) relating to certainty;
Style disjuncts . . na (b) relating to evaluation.
Many style disjuncts can be seen as abbreviated clauses in which the Both can be expressed by a wide range of adverb phrases, by prepositional
8.41
adverbial would have the role of manner adjunct: phrases and — especially those in (a) — by clauses.
(a) Certainty. These disjuncts comment on the truth value of what is
Frankly, | am tired. — - said, firmly endorsing it, expressing doubt, or posing contingencies such
(cf: ‘T tell you frankly that Tam tired’.) as Conditions or reasons. For example, beside the statement ‘The play was
Sometimes the disjunct has full clausal form: written by Francis Beaumont’, we may have:
If . I may say sno awlit hout givvaiin g you affeence, nce, | thi : nk y your wri w tin e g e i s The play was 4 aunpep ld a o r u e b n t t e ly d l . y > wri. tten by Franci.s Beaumont.
rather immature.
perhaps
More often, a clausal disjunct is nonfinite, as in fo be frank, putting il
bluntly, considered candidly. Compare also:
The semantic roles of disjuncts fall under two main heads:
(a) Manner and modality, thus involving items such as crudely, frankly, Jn essence, the judge called her evidence in question.
Since she had no time to have the car fixed, Rachel telephoned for a
honestly, truthfully; eg: taxi.
(To put it) briefly, there is nothing I can do to help. The proposal would have been accepted if the chairman had put it
You can, in all honesty, expect no further payments. more forcibly.
(b) Respect, thus involving items such as generally, literally, personally, (b) Evaluation. These disjuncts express an attitude to an utterance by
strictly; eg: way of evaluation. Some express a judgment on the utterance asa whole,
Strictly (in terms of the rules}, she should have conceded the point to
including its subject:
her opponent. Wisely, Mrs Jensen consulted her lawyer.
I would not, (speaking) personally, have taken offence at the (Mrs Jensen was wise in consulting her lawyer.”)

It will be seen that disjuncts in (b) often constitute ways of guardedly
‘hedging’ the accompanying statement. A particular case of this arises in Naturally, my husband expected me home by then.
metalinguistic comment in which the disjunct draws attention toa point of (‘It was natural for my husband to expect me back by then’ — not
“My husband was natural . . .”)

RESULTIVE, as in: Conjuncts from the same set are sometimes used in reinforcement; for
I got there very late, so I missed most of the fun. example, the additive items in:
Cfalso therefore, as a result, accordingly, in consequence, of course. Moreover, he had in addition a qualification in accountancy.

along with compatible conjunctions. These may involve coordination (as

prove of the proposal. although . . . still):

fell ill. For general studies of adverbials, see Buysschaert (1982); Greenbaum (1969):

the child became delirious.
Cf also meanwhile, originally, subsequently, eventually.

Simple and complex prepositions I like being in this reom. [1]

9.3 The commonest prepositions are a small number of monosyllabic items His life is in danger. [3]
such as at, for, in, on, to, with, typically unstressed and often with reduced They told me this in all seriousness. [4]

What are you looking af [at]? be understood as having the capacity to envelop in a kind of three-
I'm looking at [at] this huge telephone bill. dimensional space analogous to the physical room in [1]. We must
therefore begin by understanding the ways in which prepositions refer to
But in addition there are polysyllabic prepositions, some of them some of the basic spatial dimensions, as set out in Fig 9.4. This shows three
compounds formed historically from the monosyllabic ones (such as different kinds of distinction.
inside, within) or derived from participles (such as during) or adopted from
other languages (such as despite, except). Thus although prepositions are
a closed class in comparison with truly open classes like nouns, they are Positive Negative
less literally a closed class than determiners or pronouns.
The number of prepositions has been increased partly by using still
more participles (for example, barring, concerning, granted ) bul chiefly by Destination Position Destination Position
combining prepositions with other words to form ‘complex prepositions’.

First, the prepositions are contrasted as ‘posilive’ or ‘negative’ (such Tl lie on the hed for a few minutes.

distinguish between ‘destination’ (movement with respect to an intended

sion types: one which ignores dimension altogether, treating location as a directional:
point even if in reality it is a continent: She drove to Edinburgh (and arrived at noon).
He walked to the lamp-post. She drove towards Edinburgh (but that may not be her destination).

I love walking through woods in spring. [b] Prepositions that can convey motion or direction can be used also to express the
Static resultative meaning of having reached the destination:
Other prepositions commonly used for passage are by, over, wider, across,
and past. Itis worth noting the parallel between positional on and in on the The horse has successfully jumped across the gap.
one hand and across and through on the other:

196 Prepositions and prepositional phrases Prepositional meanings 197
Metaphorical extension with spatial prepositions _ . We can come on Monday or on any other day that you may prefer.
9.8 With many spatial prepositions, metaphor enables similar but abstract The baby was born on July the twelfth.
relations to be expressed. This is particularly striking with time (9.9) but a So too with an interval that is specifically part ofa day:
wide range of other meanings must also be noted, where the proximity to
literal use can be readily perceived. For example: on Sunday afternoon, on Thursday night.
in the room in this book, in some difficulty NOTE [a] Reference to night may disregard its ‘dimension’ as in:
out of the bottle out of danger, out of the competition
beneath the branches beneath contempt T have to sleep in the afternoon because 1 cannot sleep at night very casily.
under the floorboards : under suspicion But it can also be viewed as a period, with the regular use of in:
up the hill up the social scale We heard her coughing several times in the night,
downhill : down market
beyond the post office : beyond endurance {b] A phrase like in three days may be used to indicate 4 duration ora point three
over the mountain (she is getting) over her illness days hence; thus ‘He'll do it in three day? may mean either He'll lake three days to
when her temperature was over 40°C do it’ (without commitment to when the three days will be) or ‘He'll do it three days
from Boston to Bangor: from generation lo generation from now’ (the converse of ‘He did it three days ago’),
between the trees between ourselves Time duration
through the tunnel through the ordeal
9.10 Tn answer to How long? we have above all phrases with for:
NOTE Some prepositions are used in what would seem a converse relationship: We stayed in a rented cottage for the summer.
The cow is in calf. (~ 2the calf is in the cow's wan '
The office is out of envelopes. (~2All envelopes seem to be out of the office). The same meaning, with some emphasis on the duration, can be expressed
with throughout and all through. By contrast, during indicates a stretch of
Note also: injout of luck time within which a more specific duration can be indicated:
Time During the sunmer, we stayed in a rented collage for a month.
Time position . But with appropriate lexical support inthe context, the difference between
9.9 Three prepositions, af, on, and in, are used in expressions answering the during and for (throughout, etc) can be neutralized:
question ‘When?’ and they reflect a concept oft ime as analogous to space.
Thus at is used for points of time, where time is conceived as being Try to stay alert Ld th in ro in u g ghout} the entire ceremony.
‘dimensionless’:
Duration expressions with over carry the implication of a period
The film will begin af 7.20 p.m. containing some divisions or ‘fences’. Thus one can Stay overnight, over the
It is not only instants that can be so considered: weekend, over the Christmas period bul hardly *over the ceremony, *2over
the evening.
What are you doing at the weekend? Duration can be specified by reference to the beginning and ending:
She last saw her parents af Christmas.
Chaucer frequently speaks of books because at that time they were The office will be open from Monday to Friday.
rare and highly valued. While from... to corresponds to for (‘The office will be opfoer finve days’),
Where time is regarded as a period, the usual preposition is in, reflecting between... and can be used in the more general sense of during:
analogy with two- or three-dimensional space: The office will be open beriveen Mi onday and Friday. (ie ‘for a period
In the evening, I listened to some Beethoven records. within the stretch specified’),
Where did he live in his childhood?
I saw her in March/in 1988/in the following week. NOTE The expression from x to y is chicfly BrE and although it normally means that the
periods x and y are both included, there can be uncertainty. The corresponding
But in expressions referring to days, the preposition is ov: AmE expression (from) x through J} is inclusive.

498 Prepositions and prepositional phrases Prepositional meanings 199

I have been working since eight o'clock.

preposition means ‘until’:
They should cease action pending the court's decision. Compare also ‘He comes from America’ (= ‘He is an American’) in contrast to

Cause and purpose
As a subscriber, 1 ought to have been consulted.
9.12 There is a spectrum of relations extending from cause to purpose. For the
part covering cause, reason, and motive, we have prepositional phrases From means to stimulus
with items such as because of, on account of, for, out of:
9.13 Another spectrum of relations expressed by prepositions covers means
He lost his job because of his laziness. instrument, agency, and stimulus. The first two respond to the question
She was fined for dangerous driving. ow?’ with 5y used for means and with for instrument, as in:
He misdirected the letter our of spite.
They died from exposure. T go to work by car.
She can’t turn the heating on for fear of a fire outbreak. Please send this to the Delhi office by telex.
The plane crashed through some navigational error. The burglar entered by the back door.
She won the match with her fast service.
But of course the notion of ‘motive’ shades into purpose, goal, and target, He levered it up with a crowbar.
for all of which the common preposition is for:
Note the corresponding negative of with:
An appeal is being opened for a new hospital.
They are appealing for donations. He managed to open the car without a key.
We had better set out for home. In contrast to means and instrument, the agent is an animate which
She is applyinfogr a better job. instigates or causes something. It is expressed by the preposition dy, the

200 Prepositions and prepositional phrases Prepositional meanings 201
complement of which frequently corresponds to the subject ofa transitive He entered the room with a worried look.
verb: She set off without a cure.
This picture was painted by Degas.
~ Degas painted this picture. NOTE fa] Manner is also expressed with (un) like:
I was bitten by a neighbour's dog. He spoke with a barrister’s skill. (Contrast: ‘He spoke with a barrister’)
~A neighbour's dog bit me. Fred, (un jlike his brother, is trusted by few. (Contrast: ‘Fred is (un}like his
brother’)
But an agent phrase need not be accompanied by a passive verb phrase:
People thought the play was by Webster.
[b] The sense of accompaniment can shade into or even cooccur with opposition:
Stimulus and reaction are expressed chiefly by ar:
She was arguing with her brother. (cf ‘She and her brother were arguing’)
But there is also a contrast between with and againsfotr, and against:
I'm surprised at her attitude.
1 would rather have him with me than against me.
In AmB this is general, but in BrB with is preferred if the stimulus isa
Are you Uweit h } the government or agaiwng st ittye?
person:
They were furious with Paul. [c] Expressions of accompaniment are used also for possession. 80 that there is
Other stimulus and reaclion prepositions include about, in, of and to: some interchange between with(out), of, and the genitive:
I was pleased about the choice of date. The pianist has great talent, ~A pianist with great talent
Are you interested in quanti theory? ~A pianist of great talent
I think he’s jealous of her. ~ The pianist’s great talent
Ta me, her proposal is broadly acceptable. Another close tic is with the notion of ‘ingredients’, expressed by with and (out)
of:

202 Prepositions and prepositional phrases Modification 203
But for me, the case would have been lost. (fe ‘If it hadn’t been for Bibliographical note
me’)
For general and theoretical studies of prepositions, see Bennett (1975); Vester-
For the relationship respect, we have the prepositions as for, as to, about, gaard (1977). Guimier (1981) provides a valuable bibliography.
on the matter of, concerning, as regards, with regard to, and the more On specific issues, see Buyssens (1987); Jacobsson (1977); Jaworska (1986);
formal with reference to, with respect to, and re. Leech (1969); Lindkvist (1976); Quirk (1968, Ch. 14).
Now, about your application, are you sure you would like a job of
this kind?
The coat is splendid as to the material, but 'm less happy as regards
the cut.
The pair as to and asf or differ in that the latter tends to introduce a topic
transition:
The coat is splendid but as for the hat | don’t think it suits you.
Many expressions of respect occur in complementation of verbs and
adjectives (Chapter 16):
He told her of his problems but was silent on his failed marriage.
They argued about the children and quarrelled over money.
One aspect of respect is to make explicit a standard by which assessment is
made; for this, several prepositions are in common use:
For a teacher, he seems extraordinarily ignorant.
I’m better af squash than I am as a tennis player.
She is very clever with her hands.
Modification
9.16 Both prepositions and prepositional adverbs (9.2 Note [b]) can be
modified in terms of measure and degree by being accompanied by
intensifiers (7.33). For example:
She arrived { shortly after ten.
at ten or shortly after.
He expected to be well ahead of Compton but he finished in fact just
behind.
Please rehang the picture about halfa metre further down (the wall).
Though in many cases such modification seems clearly to apply to the
preposition, there are equally cases where it seems to apply to the whole
prepositional phrase:
Should I stand at one side or right in the middle?
Many people are against public ownership completely.

Syntactic functions of clause elements
Through the multiple class membership of verbs, ambiguities can arise:
She made a good model. — SVO or SVC
Objects and complements
1 found her an entertaining partner.
- SVOC or SVOO
10.5 There are two subcategories each of object and complement. The two
He is cooking his family dinner. — SVO or SVOO
types of object can cooccur:

In [2] the adverbial is subject-related (like the subject complement in (ID, object complement (cf 10.5);
and in [4]  it is object-related (like the object complement in [3]). The (d)  does not have a corresponding passive subject;
parallel is further evident in the verb classes, and we therefore call the verb (e)  in finite clauses requires the subjective form of pronouns in formal use
in both [1] and [2] copular (since it is equivalent in function to the copula (especially in AmE), but otherwise the objective form.
be) and call the verb in both [3] and [4] complex-transitive (cf 16.24/7). The ADVERBIAL (cf Chapter 8):
(a)  is normally an adverb phrase, prepositional phrase, or clause, but can

They married young. The typical role oft he direct object is that of the AFFECTED participant: a

Sometimes there is a close connection between the verb and the final element: denoted by the verb, but is directly involved in some other way:
James sold his digital watch yesterday.
The sun shone bright.

Subjects
benefit (from), as is indicated by the following relation:
Subject as external causer, instrument, and affected
Mr Smith has given his son a radio. [So now his son has.a radio.]

The perceptual verbs see and hear require an experiencer subject, in
|     | e x | p r e s se | s  t h e   u | n w i t t in | g   (g e n | er a ll y  | i n a n i m | a te )   c | a us e   o | f  a n  e v e | n t : |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | ---------- | ------------ | ------------ | ---------- | ---------- | ----------- | ---------- | ---------- | ------------- | ----- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
contrast to ook ai and listen to, which are agentive. The other perceptual
The avalanche destroyed several houses. verbs taste, smell, and feel have both an agentive meaning corresponding
The electric shock killed him.
to /ook at, as in [1], and an experiencer meaning corresponding to see, asin
[2]:
Tt may also have the role of INSTRUMENT; that is, the entity (generally
inanimate) which an agent uses to perform an action or instigate a process: I want you to taste the soup.
[1]
I can taste the pepper in my soup.
|     |     | A car knocked them down. |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | [2] |
| --- | --- | ------------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
The computer has solved the problem. The soup in (3] has the affected role:
With intransitive verbs, the subject also frequently has the AFFECTED
The soup tastes good.
|     | role elsewhere typical of the direct object: |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | [3] |
| --- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Verbs indicating cognition or emotion may also require an experiencer:
Jack fell down (accidentally).
The pencil was lying on the table. I thought you were mistaken.
T liked the play.
The term ‘affected’ can also be applied to subjects of copular verbs:
Normally, recipient and experiencer subjects go with verbs used statively.
The pencil was on the table.

Locative, temporal, and eventive subjects
A COGNATE object is similar to a resultant object in that it refers to an
10.13 The subject may have the LocaTive role of designating the place of the event indicated by the verb:
state or action, or the TEMPORAL role of designating its time:
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | Chris will sing a song for us.  |     |     |     |     | She lived a good life. |     |     |

Los Angeles is foggy. [‘It’s foggy in Los Angeles.’]

|     | A RESULTANT (or ‘effected’) object is an object whose referent exists only |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | verb is give: |           |              |             |              |                       |                 |                           |                    |
