214 The simple sentence
Subject-verb concord 215
NOTE The object may occasionally be instrumental (cf 10.10): Nominal relative clauses, on the other hand, resemble noun phrases in this
We employ a computer for our calculations. respect (cf 15.7/) and may have plural as well as singular concord: What
She is playing the piano. was once a palace is now a pile of rubble; What ideas he has are his wife's.
He nodded his head.
NOTE [a] It is possible to generalize the rule of concord to ‘A subject which is not clearly
Summary semantically plural requires a singular verb’; that is, to treat singular as the
10.18 Although the semantic functions of the elements (particularly S and Oy) o u f n m pl a u r r k a e l d it y f o i r s m p , r e to s e b n e t . u s T e h d i s in w n o e u u l t d ra e l x ci p r l c a u in m , st in a n a c d e d s, i t w io h n e r t e o n c o l a p u o s s a it l iv s e u b in je d c i t c s a , ti t o h n e
are quite varied, there are certain clear restrictions, such as that the object tendency in informal speech for is/was to follow the pseudo-subject there in
cannot be agentive; a subject (except in the passive) cannot be resultant; an existential sentences (¢f 18.30) such as ?There’s hundreds of people on the waiting
indirect object normally has only two functions — those of recipient and list and in sentences such.as ? Where's the scissors?: ? Here's John and Mary. As
alfected. The following system of priorities generally obtains: what precedes the subject here is not marked for plural, the singular verb follows
by attraction.
If there is an agentive, external causer, or positioner, it is S; if not, On the other hand, the principle of proximity (cf 10.20) effects a change from
If there is an instrument, it is S; if not, singular to plural more often than the reverse, and this perhaps suggests that we
If there is an affected item, it is S; if not, should regard the plural as the unmarked form.
If there is a temporal, locative, or eventive item, it may be S; if not, [b] Apparent exceptions to the concord rule arise with singular nouns ending with
The prop word it is S. an apparent plural -s (measles, billiards, mathematics, ete, cf 5.43) or conversely
plural nouns lacking the inflection (cartle, people, clergy, ele, cf 5.44):
Naturally, the role of the direct or indirect object in an active clause is
assigned to the subject in passive clauses. Measles is sometimes scrious.
The semantic functions of clauses are treated in Chapter 15, Our people are complaining.
[c] Plural phrases (including coordinate phrases) count as singular if they are used
as names, titles, quotations, etc:
Crime and Punishment is perhaps the best constructed of Dostoyevsky’s
novels, but The Brothers Karamazov is undoubtedly his masterpiece,
Subject—verb concord ‘Senior citizens’ means, in common parlance, people over sixty.
The titles of some works that are collections of stories, cic, may be counted as
General rule either singular or plural:
10.19, The most important type of concord in English is concord of 3rd person The Canterbury Tales {eOmXIS ISk)i.n many manuscri.pts.
number between subject and verb. A singular subject requires a singular
verb:
Notional concord and proximity
My daughter watches television after supper.
10.20 Two factors sometimes conflict with GRAMMATICAL CONCORD as described
A plural subject requires a plural verb: in 10.19, NOTIONAL CONCORD is agreement according to the idea of number
My daughters warch television after supper. rather than the presence oft he grammatical marker for that idea. In BrE,
for example, collective nouns such as government are often treated as
The number ofa noun phrase depends on the number of its head: plural (cf 10.21): The government have broken all their promises.
The change in male attitudes is most obvious in industry. The principle of proximity denotes agreement of the verb with a noun
The changes in male attitude are most obvious in industry. or pronoun that closely precedes it in preference to agreement with the
head of the noun phrase:
For coordinated noun phrases, see 10.22/f.
Clauses as subject (cf 15.1) count as singular for number concord: ?No one except his own supporters agree with him.
How you got there doesn’t concern me, Smoking cigarettes is dangerous to One in ten take drugs.
your health. The same applies to prepositional phrases and adverbs Attraction of number through proximity occurs mainly in unplanned
functioning as subject: Jn the evenings is best for me; Slowly does it!. discourse.

218 The simple sentence

Either the Mayor or her deputy is bound to come. [1] Every member of the vast ¢iras

When conjoins differ in number, recourse is generally made to the Although these sentences with plural verbs might well be uttered in casual
principle of proximity: the number of the second conjoin determines the speech, or inadvertently written down, most people would probably
number of the verb, as in [3] and [4]: regard them as ungrammatical, because they flatly contradict grammati-
Either your brakes or your eyesight is at fault. [3] cal concord. Other, more acceptable, instances arise with expressions
Either your eyesight or your brakes are at fault. [4] involving quantity (where the singular would seem pedantic):
In less formal usage, phrases coordinated with neither . . . nor are treated A (large) number of people fave applied for the job.
more like and for concord. Thus, [5] is more natural in speech than [6]: The majority agree with me.
Neither he nor his wife fave arrived. [5] NOTE The following illustrates an idiomatic anomaly: there is a discrepancy in number
Neither he nor his wife Aas arrived. [6] between the noun and the determiner ose, as well as with the verb:
When or is used for coordinative apposition (¢f 10.22), grammatical TThheessee Jisnordt orp cs are dangerous¢, al
concord requires the verb to agree in number with the first appositive: ki of partics are dangerous. Cinformal
type
Gobbledygook, or the circumlocutions of bureaucratic language, is
intentionally difficult to understand. Rephrasing can avoid the anomaly:
The circumlocutions of bureaucratic language, or gobbledygook, Those kinds of parties are
are intentionally difficult to understand. That kind of party is dangerous,
Parties of that kind are
Indefinite expressions as subject
10.25 Some indefinite pronouns and determiners have both noncount and count Concord of person
uses. With noncount nouns (present or implied), the verb is singular:
10.27 In addition to number concord, there is concord of person in the present
Some (of the cement) is arriving later today. tense:
So far ne money has been spent on repairs.
Jam your friend. [1st PERSON SINGULAR CONCORD]
With plural nouns (present or implied), the verb is plural:
He is your friend.
He knows you \BBrd PERSON SINGULAR CONCORD]
No people of that name /ive here.
Some (of the books) are being placed on the shelves today.
In the past tense, only the verb Bg has distinctions of person:
The pronouns either and neither generally take a singular verb:
I was your friend.
He was your friend {Ist and 3rd PERSON SINGULAR CONCORD]
The two guests have arrived { and eit . her } is welcome. Was .
O8 > Ubut aeither
You were my friend. [2nd PERSON]
' With none, the plural verb is more frequently used than the singular,
In a coordinate subject noun phrase where the coordinator is or or nor,
because of notional concord, even without the effect of the proximity
the last noun phrase determines the person of the verb, in accordance with
principle:
the principle of proximity:
None (of the books) are being placed on the shelves today.
Neither you, nor I, nor anyone else knows the answer. [1]
10.26 The proximity principle may lead to plural concord even with indefinites (?)Either my wife or I am going. [2]
such as each, every, everybody, anybody, and nobody (or indefinite phrases
such as every one, any one), which are otherwise unambivalently singular: NOTE {a] Because of the awkwardness of the choice in [1] and especially [2], a speaker
may avoid it by using a modal auxiliary which is invariable for person (eg: Either
Nobody, not even the teachers, {w as } li. steni.ng. my wife or I will be going) or by postposing the last noun phrase (eg: Either my wife
were is going or I am).

| (ii)  They  can  | be  followed  | by  negative  | tag  clauses,  | with  additive |               |                         |     |     |     |
Scarcely ever has Britain suffered so much criticism.
meaning:
|     |     |     |     |     | Verbs, adjectives,  | and  prepositions with  | negative  | meaning may  | be  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------- | ----------------------- | --------- | ------------ | --- |
They aren’t ready, and neither are you.
followed by nonassertive items (cf 10.37):
(iit)  They can be followed by negative agreement responses:
He denies 1 ever told him.
A: He doesn’t know Russian. B: No, he doesn’t. We were unaware of any hostility.
I’m against going out anywhere tonight.
(iv)  They can be followed by nonassertive items (cf 10.37):
He won't notice any change in you, either. [a] On/y is to some extent negative. When it focuses on a subject noun phrase, the
NOTE
latter is followed by nonassertive items: Only two of us had any experience in
Other kinds of clause negation
|     |     |     |     |     | sailing. And when it focuses on a  | fronted initial clement rather than the subject, il |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
Words negative in form and meaning may occasionally (but necd not) take subject—operator inversion: Only on Sundays
10.35 We sometimes have a choice between verb negation and negation of some do they eat with their children.
[b] Rarely may be positive when placed initially as an adjunct (¢f 8.13), in which
other element:
case it does not cause subject—operator inversion: Rarely, crime pays well, [On
An honest man would not lie. No honest man would lie. rare occasions, crime pays well.’]
| That was no? an accident. |     |     | That was no accident. |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------- | --- | --- | --------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Nonassertive items
| He wouldn’t say a word.  |     |     | He would say nota word. |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------ | --- | --- | ----------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Clause negation is frequently followed (not necessarily directly) by one or
| I won't make that mistake  |     |     | T will never make that mistake |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| -------------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
ever again.  again. more nonassertive items. The following examples illustrate the range of
these items, which may be determiners, pronouns, or adverbs:
The scope of negation (cf 10,38) is frequently different. For example,
Many people did not come to the party implies the absence of many people, ASSERTIVE NONASSERTIVE
whereas Not many people came to the party implies the presence of few We've had some Junch. We haven't had ay lunch.
people. I was speaking to somebody. I wasn’t speaking to anybody.
|                                                     |     |     |             |           | They'll finish it somehow. |     | They won’t finish it af all. |     |     |
| --------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | ----------- | --------- | -------------------------- | --- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- |
| In formal style, the negative element may often be  |     |     | moved from  | its usual |                            |     |                              |     |     |
He doesn’t ever visit us.
position to initial position, in which case there is inversion of subject and He sometimes visits us.
operator: He’s still at school. He’s not at school any /onger.
|     |     |     |     |     | Her mother’s coming, too. |     | Her mother’s not coming either. |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------- | --- | ------------------------------- | --- | --- |
Not a word would he say. I like her a great deal. I don’t like her much.
Never will I make that mistake again.
In many instances, the negative particle and the nonassertive form can
Words negative in meaning but not in form
combine to produce a negative form (no! ev~ eneverr)  or can be replaced
10.36 Several words are negative in meaning but not in form. They include: by a negative form (ot anyw~ hnoewhreree).
seldom, rarely
[a] The primary difference between some and any (and between the some- and any-
scarcely, hardly, barely
compounds) is that seme is generally specific, though unspecified, while aay is
| little, few  | (in contrast to the positive a little and a few) |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
nonspecific. That is, seme implies an amount or number that may be known Lo the
speaker. This difference tends to correlate with the difference between positive and
| They can effect clause  | negation,  | inducing  | the characteristic syntactic |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ----------------------- | ---------- | --------- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
negative contexts:
features of clause negation (cf 10.34):
They scarcely seem to care, do they? I have some money on me. [a specific, though unspecified amount of moncy]
I don’t have any money on me. [an unspecified, and also nonspecific amount
I hardly have any friends, and neither do you.
of money]
| A: Crime rarely pays.  |     | B: No, it doesn't. |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------------------- | --- | ------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
I sefdom get any sleep, either. [b] Nonassertive items appear in other contexts: questions (11.4/); conditional
|     |     |     |     |     | clauses (15.19 Note [a]); comparative clauses (15.36);  |     |     | putative s/ould-clauses |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | ----------------------- | --- |
Few members have ever attended the annual general meeting.
|     |     |     |     |     | (14.14);  restrictive relative clauses within  |     | generic  noun  | phrases,  which  | have |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------------------------------------------- | --- | -------------- | ---------------- | ---- |
When positioned initially, the adverbs normally cause subject—operator conditional meaning (Students who have any complaints should raise their hands, “If
| inversion (cf 10.35): |     |     |     |     | students have any complaints . . .’). |     |     |     |     |

228  The simple sentence
Negation  229
I did Nor offer her some chocolates. [‘It is not true that I offered her some
|     | chocolates."] |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | AUXILIARY NEGATION |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
may not [=‘permission’]
The same effect is achieved by focus on a negative operator:
You may not go swimming, [“You are not allowed to .. .’]
T pipn’r, offer her some chocolates.
cannot, can’t [in all senses]
or some other negative word:
You can’t be serious. [‘It is not possible that .. .7]
I NEVER offered her some chocolates.
You can’t go swimming, [‘You are not allowed to... .’]
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | He can’t ride a bicycle, [‘He is not able to. .  |     |     | .’] |     |

On negation, see Bolinger (1977, Chs. 2 and 3); Horn (1978a); Jackendoff (IV) EXCLAMATIVES are sentences which have an initial phrase intro-
(1969); Jespersen (1917); Klima (1964); Stockwell ct al. (1973, Ch. 5); Tottic (1977, duced by whar or how, usually with subject-verb order (cf 11.20):
1980).
What a fine watch he received for his birthday!
Associated with these four types are four major classes of discourse
functions:
(a) STATEMENTS are primarily used to convey information.
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | (b) QUESTIONS are primarily used to seek information ona  |     |     |     | specific point. |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --------------- |
(c) DIRECTIVES are primarily used to instruct somebody to do something.
(d) EXCLAMATIONS are primarily used for expressing the extent to which
the speaker is impressed by something.
NOTE
[a] Direct association between syntactic type and discourse class is the norm, but
the two do not always match. For example, a declarative question (cf 11.7) is
syntactically a declarative but semantically a question, and a rhetorical question
(cf 11.13) is syntactically an interrogative but semantically a statement.

232 Sentence types and discourse functions Questions 233
ib] We can make many more refined distinctions in the use of sentences, For Was he a famous actor in THOSE days?
example, /t's going to rain any minute now and I'm sorry about the delay are both (‘I know he was once a famous actor — but was it then or later?’]
Statements, but the first can be used to make a prediction and the second to make Was he a FAMous actor in those days?
an apology; Coyuou plledase make less noise? is a question intended as a request, [I know he was an actor in those days — but was he a famous onc?’}
whereas Do you want another cup? is a question that may be intended as an offer;
Make your bed at once and Make yourself at home are both directives, but the first Positive yes—no questions
has the force ofa command and the second the force of an invitation. 11.4 Like negative statements, yes—no questions may contain nonassertive
items such as any and ever (cf 10.37). The question containing such forms
is generally neutral, with no bias in expectation towards a positive or
negative response.
Questions
STATEMENT QUESTION
Someone called last night. Did anyone call last night?
Major classes
The boat has left already. Has the boat left yes?
11.2 Questions can be divided into three major classes according to the type of But questions may be CONDUCIVE, /e they may indicate that the speaker
reply they expect:
is predisposed to the kind of answer he has wanted or expected. Thus, a
1 Those that expect affirmation or negation, as in Have you finished the positive question may be presented in a form which is biased towards a
book?, are YES~NO QUESTIONS.
positive answer, It has positive orientation, for example, ifit uses assertive
2 Those that typically expect a reply from an open range of replies, as in forms rather than the usual nonassertive forms:
What is your name? or How old are you?, are WH-questions.
3 Those that expect as the reply one of two or more options presented in Did someone call last night? (‘Is it true that someone called last
the question, as in Would you like to go for a WALK or stay at HOME?, are night?’]
ALTERNATIVE questions. Has the boat left already?
Yes—no questions Negative pes~no questions
Form of yes—no questions 11.5 Negative questions are always conducive. Negative orientation is found in
questions which contain a negative form of one kind or another:
11.3 Yes—no questions are usually formed by placing the operator before the
subject and giving the sentence a rising intonation: Don’t you believe me? Have they never invited you home?
Hasn’the told you what todo? Has nobody called?
The boat has LErr.~ Has the boat LErr?
Negative orientation is complicated by an element of surprise or disbelief.
If there is no item in the verb phrase that can function as operator, DO is The implication is that the speaker had originally hoped for a positive
introduced, as with negation (ef 10.33):
response, but new evidence now suggests that the response will be
They live in SydneyD.o~ they live in Sydney? negative. Thus, Hasn't he told you what to do? means ‘Surely he has told
you what to do, hasn’t he? I would have thought that he had told you.’
Again as with negation, main verb Bg functions as operator; in BrE main Here there is a combining ofa positive and a negative attitude, which one
verb HAVE often acts as operator, but informally HAVE ... got is more may distinguish as the OLD EXPECTATION (positive) and NEW EXPECTATION
common:
(negative). Because the old expectation tends to be identified with the
Patrick was late. ~ Was Patrick late? speaker’s hope or wishes, negatively orientated questions often express
disappointment or annoyance:
She has a cold. ~ frees she have a cold? esp AME)
, Has she (got) a cold? <esp BrE) Can't you drive straight? [‘I'd have thought you'd be able to, but
apparently you can’t.’]
NOTE By placing the nuclear stress on a particular part of a yes—no question, we are able Aren't you ashamed of yourself? [You ought to be, but it appears
to focus the interrogation on a particular item ofi nformation which, unlike the you're not.’]
rest of the sentence, is assumed to be unknown (cf 10.39). Thus the focus falls in Hasn’t the boat left yer? [I'd hoped it would have left by now, but it
different places in the following otherwise identical questions: seems it hasn’t.’}

(ii) RISING TONE on tag  S t a te m en t  or   ex c la m a t i o n   is  p o s it iv e  or negative: isn't that so?, don’t you think?.
(iv) FALLING TONE on tag
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     | r i g h t?  C in | fo r m a l) ,  w o u l | d n 't  y o u  s a y ? |     |     |     |

He doesn’t like his OB, DOES he? He doesn’t like his 368, DOES he?
The meanings of these sentences, like their forms, involve a statement Declarative questions
and a question; each of them, that is, asserts something, then invites the
11.7 The declarative question has the form of a declarative, except for the final
listener’s response to it. Sentence (i), for example, can be rendered ‘I
rising intonation:
assume he likes his job; am I right”, (ii) means the opposite: ‘I assume he
doesn’t like his job, am I right?’. These sentences have a positive and a You've got the Tickets?
negative orientation respectively. A similar contrast exists between (ili) They’ve spoken to the amsAssador, of course?
and (iv). But itis important, again, to separate two factors: an ASSUMPTION You realize what the risks are?
Boris will be THERE, I suppose?

assertive Items (10.37):
we want to have a picnic?
He wants something to eat?
[ce] Dare is occasionally used as a nonassertive modal auxiliary, especially in BrE:
Somebody is with you? Dare we complain? Common substitutes are the main verb dare and Cesp in AME)
the blend construction with poand the bare infinitive: Do we dare to complain?;  Do
Negative questions have negative orientation, and nonassertive forms
we dare complain?
may be used following the negative:
Wh-questions
You didn’t get anything to eat?

Rhetorical questions Explicatory echo questions, which are always wh-questions, ask  for
|     |     |     |     | clarification. They have a  | falling tone on the wh-word: |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
11.13 The rhetorical question is interrogative in structure, but has the force ofa
strong assertion. The speaker does not expect an answer. A: Take a look at this! B: Take a look at WHAT?
A positive rhetorical yes-no question is like a strong negative assertion.
|     |     |     |     | A: He’s missed the bus again. |     | B: WHO's missed the bus? |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | ----------------------------- | --- | ------------------------ | --- | --- |
while a negative question is like a strong positive one.
NOTE [a] The generalized recapitulatory wh-question WHAT did you say? is sometimes
POSITIVE:
truncated to the monosyllable wHAT? (impolite except among friends), just as the
Is that a reason for desPAr? [‘Surely that is not a reason. .
.] alternative formula / beg your pardon? can be reduced simply to Pardon? Other
Can anyone doubt the wisdom of this action? [‘Surely no one can
abbreviated requests for repetition are Pardon me? (AmE), Excuse me? C(AmE).
doubt...’
and Sorry? (BrE).
NEGATIVE:
[b] What? on its own can also express general incredulity:
Isn't the answer Opvious? [‘Surely the answer is obvious.’]
|     |     |     |     | A: I paid £1,000 for that picture.  |     | B: WHAT? You must be mad. |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | ----------------------------------- | --- | ------------------------- | --- | --- |
Haven’t you got anything better to D6? (‘Surely you have something
better to do.’]
| Unlike exclamatory questions,  | these  | rhetorical  |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------------ | ------ | ----------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
questions  have  the
| normal rising intonation  | of a yes-no question, and  |     | are distinguished |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------- | --- | ----------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
chiefly by the range of pitch movement.
| There  are  also  | rhetorical  wh-questions,  | The  |     | Directives |     |     |     |     |
| ----------------- | -------------------------- | ---- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
positive  question  is
equivalent to a statement in which the wh-elementi s replaced by a negative
Directives without a subject
element:
Directives typically take the form of an imperative sentence, which differs
| Who kn6ws/cares? (‘Nobody knows/cares’ or ‘I don’t know/care.’] |     |     | 11.15 |     |     |     |     |     |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | ----- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
What pirference does it make? [‘It makes no difference. ] from a declarative sentence in that:
(i)  it generally has no subject;
How shouldf  know? [‘Therei s no reason why I should know.}
it generally has a verb in the base form.
(ii)
The less common negative question is equivalent to a statement in which
Otherwise, the clause patterns of imperative sentences show the same
the wh-element is replaced by a positive element:
range and ordering of elements as declaratives (¢f 10.1); for example;
Who bD6esn’t know? [‘Everybody knows.’]

| How  c6uLpn’t  | you  remember?  | [‘You  certainly  | should  |           |                |     |     |     |

remembered.’]
(8) VOC: Consider yourself lucky.
Rhetorical wh-questions generally have a rise—fall tone, less commonly a The imperative verb lacks tense distinction and does not allow modal
simple falling tone.
auxiliaries. The progressive formi s rare, and the perfect, even rarer:
Echo questions
Be listening to this station the same time tomorrow night.
11.14 Echo questions repeat part or all of what has been said. Replicatory echo
questions do so as a way of having their content confirmed: Passives  with  be  occur  chiefly  in  negative  directives,  where  they
generally have the meaning ‘Don’t allow yourself to be .. .:
| A: The Browns are emigrating. |     | B: Emigrating? |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ----------------------------- | --- | -------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Don’t be deceived by his looks.
| A: He’s a dermatologist.  |     | B: WHAT is he? |     |                                |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------- | --- | -------------- | --- | ------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| A: Tl pay for it.         |     |                |     | Don’t be bullied into signing. |     |     |     |     |
B: You'll WHAT?
A: Have you ever been to Valladolid?
They are less common in positive directives: Be guided by what Isay. What
B: Have I ever been WHERE? might be analysed as passives occur with get:  Get washed;  Don't get
A: She always wears a quizzical expression.
dressed yet.
B: She always wears a WHAT expression?

Don’t tell Mi to be Quiet. you be quiet!
NOTE Imperatives with /et are informally negated with don't:
Third person subjects are also possible:
Don't let’s say anything about it. <esp BrE)
Somebody open this door, Let's don’t say anything about it. <esp AmE)
Parents with children 20 to the front. Don’t let me disturb you. <esp BrE}>
Nobody move. Don't let anyone fool himself he can get away with it.
Variants occur, especially with /er’s, where not is inserted after the pronoun: Let's
not say anything about it.
Do with positive imperatives
11.19 A positive imperative can be made more persuasive or insistent (esp in
VOCATIVE:  BrE) by adding do (usually with a nuclear tone) before the verb:
MARY, play on MY side.
Play on MY side, MARY. Do have some more tea.  DO let’s go for a walk.
|     |     | SUBJECT:  |     | ‘Mary play on my side. |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

Irregular sentences  245
Exclamatives
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     | God save the Queen! |     | God forbid! |     |     |     |

11.20
Exclamatives as a formal category of sentence are restricted to the type of A less archaic formula (with subject-verb inversion) for expressing
exclamatory utterance introduced by what or how (cf 11.12). The wh-word wishes has may in front:
indicates an extreme position on some scale of value, and therefore can
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     | May the best man win!  |     |     | May you always be happy! |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------------ | --- | --- |
only appear at points where an expression ofd egree is possible: What as
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     | (ii)  There  | are  several  | kinds  | of irregular wh-questions,  |     | which  occur |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------ | ------------- | ------ | --------------------------- | --- | ------------ |
predeterminer in a noun phrase [1]; and how as intensifier of an adjective
mainly in conversation, for example:
[2] or adverb {3], or as a degree adverbial [4]. The wh-element is fronted,
but in contrast to wh-questions there is no subject—operator inversion: How about another kiss?
What about coming to my place?

What a time we've had today!
|     |     |     |     |     |     | [1] | Why all the noise? |     |     | What if it rains? |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------ | --- | --- | ----------------- | --- | --- |
How delightful her manners are!
[2]
How quickly you eat!  (iii)  Several kinds of subordinate clauses are used as sentences, generally
[3]

[4]
Sometimes, one must infer from the context whether the reference is to That I should live to see such ingratitude!
one end of the seale or the other. For example, What a time in [1] could To think that you might have been killed!
refer to a very good time ora very bad time. Well, if it isn’t Susan! [‘It is indeed Susan!"]
If only I'd listened to my parents!

11.22 Block language appears (especially in writing) in such functions as labels,
titles, newspaper headlines, headings, notices, and advertisements. Simple
block-language messages often consist of a noun phrase in isolation:

IRREGULAR sentences do not conform to the regular patterns of clause

pressure on space, and they are imitated on radio and television news
of the verb, survives in a few fossilized expressions. It is combined broadcasts. They can often be analysed in terms of clause structure, but
with subject—verb inversion (induced by the initial adverb) in, for with the omission of words that may be understood from the context, such
example: as the finite forms of the verb BE and the articles:
Long live the Republic!  So help me God. OIL SPILL THREAT DECREASING [SV]
PRESIDENT CALLS FOR CALM [SVA]
It is found without inversion in, for example:
SHARE PRICES NOW HIGHER [SAC]

246 Sentence types and discourse functions
Omissions of words that can be inferred from the context occur in other 7 2 Pro-forms and ellipsis
types of writing:
Wish you were here. [postcard]
MANUSCRIPT RECEIVED CHANGES ACCEPTED [cable]
Refrigerate after opening [label]
Motivation for abbreviation
NOTE [a] Notices of prohibition often take the form ofa noun phrase introduced by No:
No entry; No smoking.
[b] In informal conversation many types of phrases occur as complete utterances: 12.1 Pro-forms and ellipsis are syntactic devices for abbreviating constructions
The things they get up to!; You and your ideas!; Of all the stupid things to say!; to avoid redundancy. For example, we can avoid the repetition of sing
Taxi!; More coffee?; Your name?; No news. In addition there are many formulae tonight in [1] by the substitution of the pro-form do so, as in [la], or by
used for stereotyped communication situations; for example: Good morning, ellipsis (which is indicated by the symbo)l,, as in [Ib]:
Goodbye; How do you do?; Thanks; Happy Birthday.
[ec] Interjections are purely emotive words which do not enter into syntactic She might sing tonight, but I don’t think she will sing
tonight.
relations. Among the common interjections are Ah, Boo, Oh, Ouch; Sh, Wow. (1)
She might sing tonight, but I don’t think she will do so. [ta]
Bibliographical note She might sing tonight, but I don’t think she will. [Ib]
On the pragmatic functions of sentences in utterances see Austin (1962); Cole and Other things being equal, language users will follow the maxim “Reduce as
Morgan (1975); Leech (1983); Lyons (1977); Searle (1979). much as possible’.
On questions see Bolinger (1957); Hudson (1975); Pope (1976); Stenstrém The preference for abbreviation is not merely a preference for economy,
(1984); Stockwell et al. (1973, Ch. 9); on tag questions, Algeo (1990); Bald (1979); Abbreviation can contribute to clarity, since attention is focused on new
Nasslin (1984); on negative questions, Kontra (1980). information, as in [2]:
On directives see Bolinger (1967c) and (1977, Chs. 8 and 9); Downes (1977);
Stein (1976); Stockwell et al. (1973, Ch. 10). A: Have you spoken to Bob?
B: 4 Not yet,.
[2]
Recoverability
12,2 In order that constructions with pro-forms and ellipsis should be
interpreted correctly, the full form must be recoverable. We list below
three types of recoverability, and mention first the most important type
from the grammatical point of view:
(i) TEXTUAL RECOVERABILITY: The full form is recoverable from a
neighbouring part of the text.
(il) SITUATIONAL RECOVERABILITY: The full form is recoverable from the
extralinguistic situation.
(iil) STRUCTURAL RECOVERABILITY: The full form is recoverable from
knowledge of grammatical structure.
The use of the pronoun she, for example, presupposes that the speaker
and hearer know the identity of the person to whom the pronoun refers. In
[1] and [2] we can identify the referent from the linguistic context. We
understand she to refer to the same person as does the poor girl:
The poor girl did not complain, although she was badly hurt. [1]
Although she was badly hurt, rhe poor girl did not complain. [2]

248

Pro-forms
The third type of recoverability, structural recoverability, is illustrated
by the optional ellipsis of the conjunction that in [4]:
Pro-forms for noun phrases and their constituents
He admits (that) he prefers his mother’s cooking.

256 Pro-forms and ellipsis
Ellipsis 257
But whereas we can regard understand the problem as ellipted in [6], it (Do you) Want some?
would not be possible to do so in [5]:
(Have you) Got any money?
*sHE understands the problem better than H& does understand the (Is) Anything the matter?
problem. (Does) Anybody need a lift?
,
(Has) Joan finished?
Positional categories of ellipsis (Is the) Television not working?
12.15 We distinguish three categories of ellipsis according to where the ellipsis NOTE {a] Some other cases are less productive and tend to occur with certain expressions.
occurs within a construction. In rNITIAL ellipsis, the initial elements are For example, the ellipsis of the article alone in (The) Fact is I don't know what to
ellipted:
do; the ellipsis of the preposition of in (Of) Course he’s there; and ellipsis that
(I) hope he’s there. includes the initial syllable ofa word in (/ am a) 'Fraid I won't be there.
[b] In many instances of initial ellipsis, the omission may be at least partly duc to
In MEDIAL ellipsis medial elements are ellipted: subaudibility or some other process of phonological reduction.
Jill owns a Volvo and Fred (owns) a BMW.
. Structural ellipsis
And in FINAL ellipsis the final elements are ellipted:
12.17 In stRUCTURAL ellipsis, the interpretation depends on knowledge of
Tknow that we haven’t yet set the record straight, but we will (set the grammatical structure, as in the ellipsis of the conjunction that in [1] and
record straight). the preposition for in [2]:
Recoverability types of ellipsis I believe (that) you are mistaken. [1]
We’re staying there (for) another three weeks. <informal> [2]
Situational ellipsis
Many examples are confined to written language. They involve the
12.16 In SITUATIONAL ellipsis, the interpretation may depend ona knowledge of common omission of determiners, pronouns, operators, and other closed-
the extralinguistic context. For example, Get it? in one situation might be class words in block language (cf 11.22) — eg in headlines, book titles,
understood to mean the same as Did you get it? (eg: ‘Did you get the notices — and in such written varieties as lecture notes, diaries, and
letter?’) and in another situation as Do you get it? (ie: ‘Do you telegrams:
understand”). In other cases, the structure will make it clear what has
been omitted, eg it in Looks like rain. US heading for new slump. [ie The US is heading for a new slump.]
Typically situational ellipsis is initial, especially taking the form of

wh H a e t r h e a s a r b e e e s n o m e e ll ip e t x e a d m : ples of situational ellipsis, with an indication of Textual ellipsis
(a) Ellipsis in declarative sentences Categories of textual ellipsis
(1) Told you so.
12.18 In TEXTUAL ellipsis, the interpretation depends on what is said or written
(You) Want a drink, do you?
in the linguistic context. We distinguish two kinds of ellipsis according to
(It) Serves you right.
the relative positions oft he ellipsis and its antecedent: ANAPHORIC ellipsis
(I’m) Sorry I couldn’t be there.
and CATAPHORIC ellipsis. In anaphoric ellipsis, the interpretation depends
(It's) Good to see you.
on what comes before:
(Tl) See you later.
(It’s a) Pity he won’t help. I’m happy if you are (happy).
(b), Ellipsis in interrogative sentences
In cataphoric ellipsis, on the other hand, the ellipsis depends on what
(Are you) In trouble?
comes after:
(Is there) Anybody in?
Those who prefer (to stay indoors), can stay indoors.

258 Pro-forms and ellipsis
It is often necessary to take account oft he larger construction in which
the antecedent construction and elliptical construction participate, since
t S h o e m re e fo ca re te g d o is r t ie in s g o u fe is l h li p b s e i t s w a e r e e n p G os E s N ib ER le A L o n E ly L L i I n P S c I e S r , ta w in h e c r o e n s th tr e u c f t u io n n ct s i . o n W a e l
relation between the elliptical and antecedent constructions is irrelevant, t a h n a d t S r P e E l C at IA io L n E L (e LI g P S in IS , c w oo he rd re in t a h t e i o p n o ) s . s i I b n i li t t h ie is s c o h f a o p m te is r s i w on e a c r o e n d fi e n t e e r o m u i r n s e e d lv b es y
chiefly to general ellipsis, since special ellipsis is more appropriately handled in the chapters dealing with coordination (13.17), nonfinite and verbless clauses (14.47), and comparative clauses (15.36/f).
General textual ellipsis is typically final and anaphoric. We distinguish two major categories: elliptical noun phrases and antecedent clauses.
General ellipsis
Elliptical noun phrases
12.19 Except in coordination, elliptical noun phrases result from final ellipsis.
This means that heads and any postmodifiers tend to be ellipted:
My own camera, like Peter’s 4, 1s Japanese.
He had to admit that Sarah’s drawings were as good as his own Ae
The first expedition to the Antarctic was quickly followed by another
Wo a.
Tomorrow's meeting will have to be our first and our last ,. Although Helen is the oldest girl in the class, Julie is the tallest ne
Noun-phrase ellipsis, like clause ellipsis (¢f12.20//), involves some degree
of parallelism between the original construction and the elliptical
construction.
The dominance of final ellipsis in noun phrases means that itis possible
to omit postmodifiers alone:
Stan spent PART of his winnings, and the rest , he saved,
If you need any of that firewood, I can give you plenty ..
Noun phrases can occur with medial ellipsis ifa postmodifier is retained
while the head of the phrase is ellipted:
They claim that Danish butter is the Jinest in the world.
That letter was the last , I ever received Srom her,
In other cases one or more modifiers, as well as the head, may be ellipted:
His recent performance of ‘Macbeth’ is the best , he has ever done.
That new thick plastic rope that they sell is stronger than any [1]
other » you can get.
[2]
NOTE [a] In general ellipsis an elliptical noun phrase must retain more than just the
postmodifiers:
TTA
Ellipsis 259
*Joan prefers the trios of Mozart, while 1 prefer ». of Haydn.
We can make the sentence grammatical by inserting the pro-form those before of Haydn:
Joan prefers the trios of Mozart, while | prefer those of Haydn . Alternatively, we can replace the final prepositional phrase by a genitive:
Joan prefers the trios of Mozart, while | prefer Hardn’s .. {b] The elliptical phrases in [1] and [2] are ambiguous, according to whether the
modifiers are assumed to be ellipted. In [1] it may be the best performance or the best performance of ‘Macbeth’. The ambiguity is multiple in [2], where any other you can get may simply be elliptical for any other rope you can get, or the ellipsis
may include also plastic or thick plastic or new thick plastic. To avoid such
ambiguity, one has to repeat the words of the antecedent.
Elliptical clauses
Ellipsis of the predication in finite clauses
12.20 For general ellipsis in the finite clause, as in the noun phrase, the dominant
type is final. Typically, the subject and operator (and perhaps other
auxiliaries) remain, and the predication is ellipted:
I'm happy if you are ,. [1] If I could have bought a ticket, I would have 4. [2] His father was at Oxford when the Prime Minister was Ae [3]
Tom will be playing, but I don’t think Martin will (be) ,. [4]
I'll do what I can ,. [5]
When Marilyn resigns from the committee, I’m sure that a
number of other people will ,. {6]
NOTE [a] If the clause in its unreduced form has no operator, the dummy operator Do is introduced:
I left school when Dennis did. [7]
Did, however, in [7] isa pro-form and there is strictly no ellipsis, since the insertion
of the predication after did would result in an unacceptable sentence:
*T left school when Dennis cid leave school. [7a]
But there are other constructions with the operator Do that are elliptical:
I don’t like living in the country. Do you (like living in the country)? [8] A: Does she like writing for the press?
B: Yes, she des (like writing for the press). (9]
In [9] does is used as an emphatic operator in both the elliptical and the unreduced
constructions. ;
[b] Unlike adjuncts (8.13) and subjuncts (8.32). disjuncts (8.40) and conjuncts
(8.43) are not carried over to the elliptical clause. We can contrast the adjunct
sometimes with the disjunct wisely:

260 Pro-forms and ellipsis Ellipsis 261
A: Has Bob sometimes walked to work? You can borrow my pen, if you WANT = [la]
B: No, but his sister Aas. [= has sometimes walked to work] Somebody ought to help. Shall I ask Peter -? [3a]
A: Bob has wisely walked to work.
B: Well, at least he CLAIMS he has. [= has walked to work] NOTE The marginal modal auxiliaries ought to and used to, the modal idiom have got 0,
Similarly, a final auxiliary in the elliptical clause excludes other, contrasting and semi-auxiliaries such as be able to, be going to, have to, be supposed to (cf3.17/)
auxiliaries in the antecedent clause: must retain the fo:
Not many people could have enjoyed that trip as much as your mother has. We don’t save as much money these days as we used to q.
[= has enjoyed that trip] I won’t disturb you again unless I have to ,.
Medial ellipsis Appended clauses
12.21 There is genuinely medial ellipsis when a contrasting adverbial occurs in 12.23 One type of special ellipsis is found in appended clauses. An appended
final position: clause is an elliptical clause (usually parenthetical or an afterthought) for
which the whole or part of the preceding or interrupted clause constitutes
There are more hungry people in the world today than there were , the antecedent:
in 1900.
I caught the train — jusv. (1]
In the next example, only the lexical verb is omitted:
The train arrived ~ on time for a change. {2]
Pil gladly pay for the hotel, if you will a for the food.
These presuppose that two separate assertions are being made. For
Ellipsis of a clause example, [1] may be viewed as elliptical for [1a]:
12.22 A more thoroughgoing reduction involves ellipsis of the whole clause or I caught the train — I just caught the train. [la]
the whole clause except for an introductory word.
A wh-interrogative clause, whether independent or subordihate, may be In the examples that follow, only part of the initial clause (the italicized
reduced to the wh-word: part) acts as the antecedent:
A: We’re bound to win the prize some day. They are meant to wound, perhaps , to kill.
B: Yes, but wun ,? He is playful, , even mischievous.
Somebody has hidden my notebook, but I don’t know WHO/WHY/ On the related construction of appended coordination, ef 13.30.
WHERE Ae
There is also a reduced negative wh-question, but this occurs only with Bibliographical note
why and with wh-infinitive clauses: On reduction generally, see Halliday and Hasan (1976).
Why not? I don’t know why not. On pro-forms and substitutes, see Crymes (1968).
I don’t want to accept, but I don’t know how not (to). O Fo n r e f l u li r p t s h i e s, r s r e e e f e G re r n ee ce n s b s a e u e m th a e n B d ib M li e o y g e r r a p (1 h 9 ic 8 a 2 l ) : n G ot u e n s te fo r r (1 C 9 h 6 a 3 p ) t . ers 13 and 19.
A to-infinitive clause may be omitted if it functions as the complemen-
tation of a verb or adjective (cf Chapter 16). An elliptical so-infinitive
clause may consist of just the introductory unstressed particle to:
You can borrow my pen, if you WANT to Ae [1]
You will speak to who(m)ever I TELL you to ,. [2]
Somebody ought to help. Shall I ask PETER to a? [3]
In the negative, nor is placed before to:
She borrowed my pen, although I told her Nor to.
To may also be ellipted, and the result is then ellipsis of the whole clause:

(b) Coordinated clauses are sequentially fixed
The exceptions are the conjunycetr  and (in informal spoken English) the
13.5 Clauses beginning with and, or, and but are sequentially fixed in relation to
conjunct so and the time adverb ¢/en (‘after that’):
| the  previous  | clause,  | and  therefore  | cannot  | be  | transposed  |         |     |                                        |     |     |     |     |

|                |          |                 |         |     |             | without |     | They didn’t like it, yet said nothing. |     |     |     |     |
producing unacceptable sentences, or at least changing the relationship
| between the clauses: |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | They were tired, so left early. |     |     |     |     |
| -------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
They went home, tea went straight to bed.
They are living in England, or they are spending a vacation there.

coordinator:
This is true for coordinators and conjuncts, but not for most subordina-
tors.  Contrast  the  unacceptability  of [1a],  containing  *She didn't say anything about it because he was new and because looked
the  conjunct
nevertheless, with the acceptability of [1b], containing the subordinator unwell.  LH]
although:

*For he was unhappy, he asked to be transferred.
clauses:

are linked by the coordinators and, or, and but (also by for and so that), a pronoun

reference when it occurs in an initial subordinate clause: and even so it can link only certain types of subordinate clauses.

(c)  The second clause introduces @ CONTRAST: CORRECTIVE to what is said in the first conjoin:
They are enjoying themselves, or (at least)/(rather) they apPEAR to

Correlatives 269
268 Coordination
In this use, bu? can be emphasized by the conjuncts on the contrary or
(d) In addition to introducing alternatives as indicated above, or may
rather. It normally does not link two clauses, but two lesser constituents.
imply a NEGATIVE CONDITION. Thus in:
Switch on the radio or we'll miss the news.
the implication can be paraphrased by the negative conditional clause:
Switch on the radio. /f you don’t switch on the radio, we'll miss the Correlatives
news.
The conditional use of or is thus the negative analogue oft he conditional Either...or, both... and, neither... nor
use ofa nd (cf 13.11). Unlike and, however, or typically follows a negative 13.14 The three pairs either or, both... and, and neither . . . nor are correlatives.
imperative clause: The first word is an ENDORSING ITEM and the second is a coordinator.
Either . . . or emphasizes the exclusive meaning of or (cf 13.12). The
Don’t be too long, or you'll miss the bus.
linked units may be complete clauses or lesser constituents:
In this case, the most appropriate paraphrase with an i/-clause is positive
Either the room is too small or the piano is too large.
instead of negative:
You may either stand up or sit down.
If you are too long, you'll miss the bus. Either Sylvia or her sister will be staying with us.
NOTE {a] In written varieties of the language where precision is required (eg in official Both ... and emphasizes the additive meaning of and (cf 13.11):
instructions), the third possibility can be explicitly included by the use of both David both loves Joan and wants to marry her.
coordinators (usually written and/or): This new machine will both accelerate the copying process and
If the appliance is defective, write directly to the manufacturer and/or improve the quality of reproduction.
complain to your local consumer protection service. Both Mary and Peter washed the dishes.
The regulations are both very precise and very detailed.
[b] Because and and or contrast with one another in meaning, or following a
negative is in some respects equivalent to and. Thus: It also singles out the segregatory meaning of and (cf 13.23f) rather than
He doesn’t have long hair or wear jeans. [1] the combinatory meaning:
is logically equivalent to ‘He doesn’t have long hair AND He doesn’t wear jeans’. Both David and Joan got divorced. [not from each other]
Conversely:
Neither .. .nor is the negative counterpart of borh ... and. Itemphasizes
“He doesn’t (both) have long hair and wear jeans. (2] that the negation applies to both units:
is logically equivalent to ‘EITHER He doesn't have long hair or He doesn’t wear jeans David neither loves Joan, nor wants to marry her.
"(or both)’. The reversal of meaning arises because in (1] and [2], the coordinator is Mary was neither happy nor sad.
within the scope of negation (cf 10.38). Neither Peter nor his wife wanted the responsibility.
The uses of but Unlike either... or, both... and and neither... nor cannot link com-
13.13 But expresses a contrast. plete clauses:
(a) The content of the second clause is unexpected in view oft he content of
* Both Mary washed the dishes and Peter dried them.
the first:
* Neither Peter wanted the responsibility, nor his wife did.
John is poor, but he is happy.
In this use, but can be replaced by and yet.
NOTE [a] When either . . . or is within the scope of negation (cf 13.12 Note [b}), it is
equivalent to neither... nor, so that these two sentences are similar in meaning:
(b) The second clause expresses in positive terms what the negation in the
first clause conveys: He hasn't met either her mother or her father.
He has met neither her mother nor her father.
Jane did not waste her time before the exam, but (on the contrary)
studied hard every evening. [b] According to a prescriptive tradition, the use of correlative coordinators is
unacceptable when there are three or more conjoins:
I am not objecting to his morals, but (rather) to his manners.

Coordination of noun phrases and their constituents
[COORDINATED -ED PARTICIPLE CLAUSES]
Noun-phrase coordination
With George ill and (with) the children at home, Jenny is finding life
very difficult. 13.21 Two or more noun phrases may be conjoined to form a conjoint noun
[COORDINATED VERBLESS CLAUSES} phrase; for example, the conjoint noun phrases functioning as subject in
[1] and as object in [2]:

his wife and child [=/is wife and his child] child). They may also be the intermediate units called NOMINAL EXPRESSIONS (cf
old men and women [=o/d men and old women] 12.5), eg: eldest child in his wife and eldest child.
some cows and pigs from our farm [= some cows from our farm and [d] The tags aadso on, and so forth, and e1 cetera (Latin = ‘and others’, abbreviated
some pigs front our farai] ve in writing as etc) are abbreviatory devices which are added to a coordinated list. to
the boys and girls staying at the hostel [=the boys staying at the indicate that the list has not been exhaustively given:

It is also possible to interpret some of these phrases as coordinated noun Aad so on and anso fodrth (and their combination and so on and so forth) are used
phrases: in the same way, but are restricted to informal use, and tend to occur after
coordinated clauses rather than coordinated phrases. A less common phrase of the
old men and women [= women and old men]
same kind is and the like.
some cows and pigs from our farm [= pigs from our farm and some
cows] Coordination of other constituents
13.26 _All the main variations of constructions that we have noted for clauses
Coordinated modifiers and noun phrases are found in the coordination of other constituents.
13.25 Only the segregatory meaning is ordinarily possible when the coordinated Examples of the coordination of various constituents are given below:
modifiers denote mutually exclusive properties: (a) Verb phrases:
old and new furniture [= old furniture and new furniture] Good cooking can disguise, but cannot improve the quality of the
workers from France and from Italy [= workers from France and ingredients.
workers from Italy]
(b) Main verbs:
Exceptions to this are colour adjectives (as in red, white, and blue flags),
Many people might have been Ailled or injured by the explosion.
which allow the combinatory sense ‘partly one colour, partly another’. On
the other hand, only the combinatory meaning is possible if the head is a (c) Auxiliaries:
singular count noun:
The country can and must recover from its present crisis.
a dishonest and lazy student [=a student who is both dishonest and
(d) Adjective phrases:
lazy]
a book on reptiles and amphibians The journey was long and extremely arduous.
The same meaning applies when the coordination is asyndetic: a dishonest, (e) Adjective heads:
lazy student.
I'm feeling younger and healthier than I felt for years.
In other instances there may be ambiguity:
(f) Adverbs:
old and valuable books [= books that are old and valuable or old
books and valuable books] She made the announcement quietly but very confidently.
buses for the Houses of Parliament and for Victoria Station [either
(g) Prepositional phrases and prepositions:
the same bus or buses go to both places or a different bus or buses
go to each place] He spoke for the first motion but against the second motion.
She climbed wp and over the wall.
NOTE [a] The coordination of determiners (eg: these and those chairs; your and my
Part of the prepositional complement may be ellipted in the first conjoin
problems) is comparatively rare, and the synonymous construction with conjoint
or a subsequent conjoin:
noun phrases (eg: these chairs and those; your problems and mine) is preferred.
{b] Cardinal numbers are frequently coordinated with or in an idiomatic He spoke for the first . but against the second motion. (formal)
approximative function: one or two guests (‘a small number’), five or six letters He spoke for the first motion but against the second 4.
(approximately in the range of five and six’), ten or twenty students (‘a number
from ten to twenty’). ; (h) Coordination of subordinators and other clause-introducing words:
[ce] The conjoins in a conjoint noun phrase may be words (eg: his wife and child,
I am prepared to meet them when and where they like.
where the two nouns share the determiner) or phrases (eg: his wife and his
I am determined to find out who or whar caused this uproar.

|     |      |              |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | Complex coordination  |     | 279 |
The general principle governing coordination is that the conjoins must
He wears smart clothes and sometimes a yachting cap at weekends.
belong to the same category in form, function, and meaning. There may,
however, be differences in form:
Such examples are more likely to occur in informa! speech.
The enemy attacked quickly and with great force.
13.28 In the second type of complex coordination, the conjoins are not in final
You can wash them manually or by using a machine.
position:
They can call this week or whenever you wish.
Dennis was carefree and in good health.
Gregory Peck always wAs| and alvays Witt be|  her

principle at work here appears to be a principle of rhythmic regularity: eg the The second conjoin is separated by intonation in speech (as in [1]) and by

280 Coordination Quasi-coordination 281
Jane has looked more healthy, and Maurice , more relaxed. since Pll ery and come tomorrow. [= try to come]
their vacation. They’ve gone and upset her again.
They sat and talked about the old times. [=sat talking]
NOTE Coordination with gapping is more difficult to understand than coordination
(b) The coordination of two adjectives of which the first functions as an
without gapping, and therefore nongapped interpretations are more likely to be
intensifier of the second:
intended where both are possible. For example, the reading of[ I] as {la] is more
likely than as [Ib]: , This room is nice and warm. [=comfortably warm]
Barbara gave Sue a magnolia and Ada a camellia. {1} His speech was nice and short.
{= Barbara gave Sue a magnolia and Barbara gave Ada a It was lovely and cool in there.
camellia] [la]
Some speakers Cesp in AmE) use good in the same way:
[= Barbara gave Sue a magnolia and Ada gave Sue a camellia] [1b]
The road is good and long.
even where the adjectival form following and is used as an adverb;
T hit him good and hard.
Appended coordination She drove good and fast.
(c) The coordination of identical comparative forms of adjectives,
13.30 APPENDED COORDINATION, which is characteristic of informal speech, adverbs, and determiners (usually just two conjoins) that expresses a
occurs when an elliptical clause (involving one element or contiguous
continuing increase in degree:
elements) is appended to a previous clause (¢f appended clauses, 12.23):
She felt more and more angry. [= increasingly angry]
John writes extremely well — and sALly, Too.
The car went slower and slower.
My mother plays badminton, and sometimes even tennis.
He got a bike for his birthday, and a book and a pen. (d) The coordination of two or more identical forms of verbs and
His left hook could fell the champion, and indeed any other boxer in adverbs that expresses continuation or repetition:
his class.
He talked and talked and talked. [= talked for a very long time]
They knocked and knocked. [= knocked repeatedly]
NOTE [a] With or and but, appended coordination is also likely to occur in careful speech
She talked on and on and on, [= continuously]
and writing:
(e) The coordination of two identical nouns to indicate different kinds:
Tam not sure whether JANE wrote the letter, or sALly
pEter plays football, but not JOHN. There are teachers and teachers. (roughly: ‘good and bad teachers’]
[b] The second conjoin may be interpolated as a parenthesis, in which case the You can find doctors and doctors. [roughly: ‘good and bad doctors’}
structure is a type of complex coordination (ef 13.28):
(f) The coordination oft hree or more identical nouns to indicate a large
John — and Sally, too — writes extremely well. number or quantity:
She can, and probably will, beat the world record.
We saw dogs and dogs and dogs all over the place.
There was nothing but rain, rain, rain from one week to the next.
Pseudo-coordination
Quasi-coordination
13.31 There are several types of PSEUDO-COORDINATION, mostly found in
informal speech: 13.32 Most of the QUASI-COORDINATORS are related to comparative forms: as
(a) The coordination oft wo verbs that has an idiomatic function similar well as, as much as, rather than. more than. They sometimes resemble
to that of a catenative construction (cf 3.18 Note): coordinators in that they link a variety of constituents:

(3]
The sentence is a complex sentence consisting of one main clause. The
main clause is superordinate to the subordinate that-clause (which is a
direct object) that continues to the end of the sentence. The that-clause is
|     |     |     |     | in  turn  superordinate  | to             | the  subordinate  | when-clause  | (which     | is  an |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------ | -------------- | ----------------- | ------------ | ---------- | ------ |
|     |     |     |     | adverbial)  that         | extends  from  | when  to          | the  end     |            |        |
|     |     |     |     |                          |                |                   | of the       | sentence.  | The    |
hierarchy of superordination and subordination is displayed in Fig. 14.1.
For certain purposes it is useful to distinguish between a subordinate
clause and the MATRIX clause. The matrix clause is the superordinate
clause minus its subordinate clause. For example, in [4] the matrix clause is
I'll lend you some money:
|     |     |     |     | I'll lend you some money if you don’t have any money on you.  |     |     |     |     | [4] |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
NOTE Some grammarians-use main clause in the sense that we give to matrix clause.

NOTE A subordinate clause may function as a constituent ofa phrase, for example as a
relative clause acting as a postmodifier in a noun phrase:
The school which my children attend is within walking distance.
The noun phrase is complex, but we do not consider that the sentence is therefore a
complex sentence, since the subordinate clause does not function as a constituent
of the sentence.
that he would discover the tiny particle
Finite, nonfinite, and verbless clauses
subordinate clause
14.3 We recognize three main structural classes of clauses:
FINITE CLAUSE: a Clause whose verb element is finite (such as takes, took,
can work, has worked, is writing, was written; cf 3.3, 3.19); eg:
5 Vv 0
I can’t go out with you because I am studying this evening.
| |
NONFINITE CLAUSE: a clause whose verb element is nonfinite (such as to
when he conducted his next experiment
work, having worked, taken; ef 3.3, 3.20).; eg:
Fig 14.1 Superordinate and subordinate clauses
Knowing my temper, 1 didn’t reply.
VERBLESS CLAUSE: a Clause that does not have a verb element, eg:
Although always helpful, he was not much liked.
Subordination and coordination
We recognize nonfinite and verbless structures as clauses because we
can analyse their internal structure into the same functional elements that
14.2 The device of subordination enables us to construct a multiple hierarchy
we distinguish in finite clauses. Consider. for example, the analysis oft he
of clauses, one within the other, sometimes resulting in extremely involved
nonfinite clause in:
sentences. Further complexity and structural variability are provided by
the interrelation of subordination and coordination. Each main clause in Knowing [V] my temper [Og], I didn’t reply.
a compound sentence may include one or more subordinate clauses. each
The analysis depends on the analogy with the corresponding finite clause:
of which may in turn include subordinate clauses. For example, [1]
displays a compound sentence in which two main clauses are coordinated: I[S] know [V] my temper [Og].
I think that your new position demands sensitive judgments Similarly, the verbless clause although always helpful in:
and I would hope that you will mature as the years go by. [1]
Although [conj] always [A] helpful [C,], he was not much liked.

form of the verb ée, the verbless clause is limited to the two clause-types ending with optional thar:

Formal indicators of subordination
Others:
14.7 Subordination is generally marked by a signal in the subordinate clause. as if, as though, in case
The signal may be of various kinds: a subordinating conjunction, a wA-
element, the item that, subject-operator inversion in declarative clauses, Correlative subordinators
or (negatively) the absence of a finite verb.
as ++ .8O
NOTE More than one subordination signal may cooccur in the same subordinate clause.
For example, a nonfinite or verbless clause may be introduced by a subordinating S a O s \ ... a8
conjunction.
such
Subordinators
80 \. .. that
14.8 SUBORDINATORS (or, more fully, suBORDINATING CONJUNCTIONS) are the such
most important formal device of subordination, particularly for finite
less
clauses. Like prepositions, which they resemble in having a relating ... than
more (/-er)
function, subordinators forming the core of the class consist of a single
word, but there is a large range of multi-word subordinators which no sooner than ... than, when Cinformal>
function, to varying degrees, likea single conjunction. In-addition. there is barely
a small class of correlative subordinators, which combine two markers of hardly \... when, than, <informal>
subordination, one being a subordinator.
scarcely

The more usual verb forms for the putative meaning in though-clauses are the
If we had enough money, we could buy a computer. simple present indicative or putative should followed by the infinitive. Clauses of
If he had apologized, you should have done 80 too. purpose require modal auxiliaries, and therefore only the should-construction is a
possible alternative.

The present and past subjunctive wish she was not married). The present indicative is a possible alternative after as if

296 The complex sentence Direct and indirect speech 297
] prefer that she should drive. [5] The perfect with other temporal clauses
I'm anxious that I shouldn't be in the way. [6]
They’ve arranged that I should absent myself for part of 14.16 When an after-clause or a when-clause refers to a sequence of two past
the committee meeting. & [7] events, the verb in the temporal clause may be in the past perfect, though it
I can understand their eagerness that you should be the is more commonly in the simple past:
main speaker. [8] We ate our meal { after Y Ww returned } from the game.
The expressions in the matrix clause may convey an emotional reaction ¥v hen J had returned
(for example, surprise or worry), as in [1-4], or the notions of necessity, All four forms of these sentences are acceptable, and mean roughly the
plan, or intention for the future, as in [5-8]. For [5-8], the present same. The only difference is that when and the simple past (probably the
subjunctive is preferred in AmE (cf 14.13). most popular choice) suggests that the one event follows immediately on
the other in sequence. There may, however, be a contrast when the
The perfect with temporal since-clauses subordinator is when if the predication in the when-clause is durative:
14.15 A temporal since-clause generally requires the present perfect. in the They walked out when I { ghaavde given} the lecture.
matrix clause when the whole construction refers to a stretch of time up to
(and potentially including) the present: The variant with the simple past would normally mean ‘as soon as I
I have lost ten pounds since I started swimming. [1] started giving the lecture’ or ‘during the time I was giving the lecture’,
Since leaving home, Larry Aas written to his parents just once. [2] whereas that with the past perfect means ‘after the lecture was over’.
The present perfect is common in temporal and conditional clauses
Ininformal AmE, and increasingly in informal BrE, nonperfect forms are when the clauses refer to a sequence of future events:
commonly used in the matrix clauses; for example, Jost instead of have lost
in [1], and wrote instead of have written in [2]. When theyve’ scored their next goal. we'll go home.
When the whole construction refers to a stretch of time up to (and As soon as I’ve retired, I'll buy a cottage in the country.
potentially including) the present, the verb in the since-clause may be the After they have left, we can smoke.
simple past or the present perfect. The simple past is used when the since- If I've written the paper before Monday, I'll call you.
clause refers to a point of time marking the beginning of the situation: In each case, the simple present is an alternative.
She has been talking since she was one year old.
Since I saw her last, she has dyed her hair. NOTE These four sentences seem to be equivalent in meaning:
Derek hasn’t stopped talking since he arrived. I saw him before he saw me. {]
The present perfect is used in both clauses when the since-clause refers to a I I s h a a w d s h e i e m n b h e im fo r b e e h fo e r e ja h d e s s e a e w n m m e e . . [ [ 3 2 ] ]
period of time lasting to the present: I had seen him before he had seen me. [4]
Max has been tense since he’s been taking drugs. Sentence [3] appears to be paradoxical in that the second in the succession of
Since I have been here, I haven’t left my seat. events is marked with the past perfect. One explanation is that the before-clause in
Since I've known Caroline, she has been interested in athletics. [3], and perhaps also in [4], is nonfactual: ie “He did not geta chance to see me’. But
Pve had a dog ever since I’ve owned a house. it is also possible that the meaning of the subordinator before has influenced the
I’ve gone to concerts ever since I’ve lived in Edinburgh. use of the past perfect as one of the choices of verb forms. or possibly there is an
analogy with the use of the past perfect in an after-clause.
When the whole period is set in past time, the past perfect or the simple
past is used in both clauses:
Since the country (had) achieved independence, it (had) revised its
constitution twice. Direct and indirect speech
Since he had snowy her, she {hed been } a journalist. 14.17 DIRECT SPEECH purports to give the exact words that someone utters or has
knew
uttered in speech or writing. INDIRECT SPEECH, on the other hand, conveys

pronoun, and the reporting clause is medial. It is unusual and archaic or

even when the verb is said (eg: said he). She said that they are being discriminated against.

Sylvia thinks Paul went to Lancaster last night.
14.21 If there is a change in time-reference, a modal auxiliary is backshifted
from present tense forms to past tense forms even if these do not normally
Other changes in indirect speech indicate past time in direct speech:
14.19  The reference to persons in indirect speech must be appropriate to the
“You may be able to answer this question,’ he told her.
situation at the time of reporting. There may therefore be changes in
~ He told her that she might be able to answer that question.
pronouns or nouns, as illustrated below: ‘T won't pay another penny,’ I said.
~T said that I wouldn’t pay another penny.
‘Pll behave myself, he promised.
~ He promised that he’d behave himself. Ifa modal auxiliary in the direct speech is already in the past tense form,
‘You know my family,’ she said. then the same form remains in the indirect speech:
~ She told Aint [or Tom, for example] that Ae knew her family.
“You shouldn't smoke in the bedroom,’ he told them.
Other changes may be necessary to adjust the references to time or ~ He told them that they shouldn't smoke in the bedroom.
| p l a c e . |   F o r  example, yesterday to last  |     |     | Monday, now to then, here to there or |     |     |     |     |     |     |

Several modal auxiliaries or marginal modals have only one form: must,
a t   c o l l e g e .
ought to, need, and had better. That form remains in indirect speech:
“You must be hungry,” he said.
Indirect statements, questions, exclamations, directives
~ He said that they must be hungry.
14.20  All the main discourse types may be converted into indirect speech.
‘You had better not say anything about this,’ he warned me.
~ He warned me that I had better not say anything about that.

I sat on the grass staring at the passers-by. Everybody seemed in a

Transferred negation
14.23 TRANSFERRED NEGATION, particularly common in informa! style, is the
transfer of the negative from a subordinate clause, where semantically it
belongs, to the matrix clause. don’t think it 'S.@ good idea is an example of
transferred negation, since it can be understood as virtually synonymous
with J think it isn't a good idea.
The matrix verbs that allow transferred negation convey notions of
opinion (eg: believe, expect, imagine, suppose, think) or perception (eg:
appear, seem, feel as if, look as if. sound as if). Here are some examples;
I don’t believe I’ve met you before. [‘I believe I haven’t met you
before’]
She didn’t imagine that we would say anything. [‘She imagined that
we wouldn’t say anything’]
He didn’t expect to win. [He expected not to win’]
It doesn’t seem that we can get our money back. [‘It seems that we
can’t get our money back’]
ae The baby doesn’t appear to be awake. [‘The baby appears not to be
awake’]
ay
: It d ra o i e n s ’] n’t look as if it’s going to rain. [‘It looks as if it isn’t going to
H ig i NOTE When the subject of the main clause is /, the tag question corresponds with the
subordinate clause:
I don’t imagine he CARES, DOES he? [I imagine he doesn’t CARE, DOES he?]

Nominal clauses 305
1 5 Syntactic and semantic functions of disjuncts (8.40). In those functions they are like adverb phrases, but in
their potentiality for greater explicitness. they are more often like
subordinate clauses prepositional phrases:
We left after the speeches ended.
We left after the end of the speeches.
RELATIVE CLAUSES generally function as restrictive or nonrestrictive
Syntactic functions of subordinate clauses modifiers of noun phrases and are therefore functionally parallel to
attributive adjectives. Compare:
15.1 Subordinate clauses may function as subject, object, complement, or
aman who is lone~lya lonely man
adverbial in a superordinate clause:
But they are positioned like postmodifving prepositional phrases:
Subject: That we need a larger computer has become obvious.
Direct object: He doesn’t know whether to send a gift. tourists who come from Italy~ tourists from Italy
Indirect abject: You can tell whoever is waiting that Pll be back in ten
These relative clauses are discussed in Chapter 17. Two types of relative
minutes,
clauses, however, are treated in this chapter: nominal relative clauses
Subject complement: One likely result of the postponement is thar
(15.7/) and sentential relative clauses (15.33)
the cost of constructing the college will be very much higher.
COMPARATIVE CLAUSES (cf 15.36/7) resemble adjectives and adverbs in
Object complement: I know her to be reliable.
their modifying functions:
Adverbial: When you see them, give them my best wishes.
She has more patience than you have.
In addition, subordinate clauses may function within these elements, eg:
He’s not as clever a man as J thought.
Postmodifier in noun phrase: (Few of the immigrants retained) the I love you more deeply than J can say.
customs that they had brought with them.
Semantically, the comparative clauses together with their correlative
Prepositional complement: (It depends) on what we decide.
element (eg: more, as, -er) are equivalent to degree adverbs.
Adjectival complementation: (We are) happy fo see pou.
NOTE There are constraints on the functioning of clauses as indirect objects or as object
NOTE Unlike noun phrases, nominal clauses may also function as adjective complemen-
tation without a preposition (¢f 16.39):
complements. Among the finite clauses, only nominal relative clauses (cf 15.7/)
function as indirect object or as object complement. I’m not sure that J can remember the exact details.
Functional classes of subordinate clauses
15,2 On the basis of their potential functions, we distinguish four major Nominal clauses
categories of subordinate clauses: NOMINAL, ADVERBIAL, RELATIVE. and
COMPARATIVE.
That-clauses
Like noun phrases NOMINAL CLAUSES (cf 15.3//) may function as subject.
object, complement, appositive, and prepositional complement. But the 15.3. Nominal that-clauses may function as:
occurrence of nominal clauses is more limited than that of noun phrases,
Subject: That the invading troops have been withdrawn has not
because semantically the clauses are normally abstract; ie they refer to
affected our government’s trade sanctions.
such abstractions as events, facts, and ideas. The one exception is the
Direct object: I noticed that he spoke English with an Australian
nominal relative clause, which may refer to persons and things and may in
accent.
fact be alternatively analysed as a noun phrase (cf 15.7). Since indirect
Subject complement: My assumpiion is that interest rates will soon
objects normally refer to persons, we can see why only the nominal
. fall, -
relative clause can function as indirect object. Nominal clauses involved in
Appositive: Your criticism, tha: no account has been taken of

psychological factors, is fully justified.

The nominal to-infinitive often indicates that its proposition is 4
possibility or a proposal rather than something fulfilled, and it is then A nominal -ing clause may refer to a fact or an action:
closest semantically to a that-clause with putative should (cf 14.14):

Other types of nominal to-infinitive clauses are treated elsewhere: wh-
interrogative clauses (15.4), yes—no and alternative interrogative clauses NOTE [a] The -ing participle in a nominal -ing clause is commonly called a ‘gerund’.
(15.5), and nominal relative clauses (15.7/). [b] Extraposition is less common with the -ing participle and often seems like an
