clause in AmE:
Bare infinitive clauses
He didn’t like me to be alone at night,
He didn’t like for me to be alone at night. <AmE). 15.11 The most common functions of the nominal bare infinitive clause are as
subject or subject complement in a pseudo-cleft sentence (or a variant of
it, ef 18.20), where the other subordinate clause has the substitute verb po:
-ing clauses
What the plan does is (to) ensure a fair pension for all,
15.10 Nominal -ing clauses may function as: Turn off the tap was all I did.
Subject: Watching television keeps them out of mischief. The ¢o of the infinitive is optional when the clause is subject complement.
Direct object: He enjoys playing practical jokes.
Subject complement: Her first job had been selling computers. NOTE A bare infinitive clause may function as object complement with a relatively few
Appositive: His current research, investigating attitudes to racial superordinate verbs (cf 16.28):
stereolypes, takes up most of his time.
They made her pay for the damage.
Adjectival complementation: They are busy preparing a barbecue.
Tt may follow prepositions of exception (cf9.13):
If the -ing clause has a subject, the subject may be in the genitive case or
it may be in the objective case (for those pronouns having an objective She did everything but make her bed.
case) or common case (for all other heads of noun phrases):
Verbless clauses
GENITIVE: I object to his/Jeremy’s receiving an invitation.
OBJECTIVE: I objected to him/Jeremy receiving an invitation. 15.12 The nominal verbless clause is a more debatable category than the other
nominal clauses.
There is a traditional prescription in favour of the genitive: it is preferred if
the subject is a pronoun, the noun phrase has personal reference, and the A friend in need is a friend indeed. [proverb] [1]
style is formal. The genitive is also preferred if the subject is initial in the Wall-to-wall carpets in every room is their dream. [2]
sentence. Are bicycles wise in heavy traffic? [3]
My forgetting her name was embarrassing. These may be paraphrased:
On the other hand, the common case is preferred where the subject is a To be a friend in need is to be a friend indeed. [la]
nonpersonal noun phrase and not a pronoun and the style is not formal: Having wall-to-wall carpets in every room is their dream. [2a]
Is it wise to have bicycles in heavy traffic? [3a]
I don’t know about the weather being so awful in this area.
The paraphrases suggest a clausal analvsis. Note also the apparent
The genitive is avoided when the noun phrase is lengthy and requires a semantic anomaly of bicycles are wise (implying wise bicycles), and the
group genitive (cf 17.26): singular verb in [2].

316 Syntactic and semantic functions of subordinate clauses
Adverbial clauses 317
Several temporal subordinators may have primarily a place meaning in You may leave the apartment at any time, provided that you give a
descriptions of scenes, when the scenes are described dynamically in terms
month's notice or pay an additional month’s rent.
of movement from one place to another:
In case you want me, Tl be in my office till lunchtime.
Take the right fork when the road splits into two. Given that x=y, then n(x+a)=n(y+a) must also be truc.
The river continues winding watil it reaches a large lake. <in formal argumentation)
The building becomes narrower as it rises higher. Assuming that the movie starts at eight, shouldn’t we be leaving now?
The road stops just after it goes under a bridge. Unless otherwise instructed, you should leave by the back exit,
Once the mountains rise above the snow line, vegetation is sparse. Marion wants me to type the letter if possible.
If not, | can discuss the matter with you now.
NOTE [a] Where-clauses may combine the meanings of place and contrast:
Where I saw only wilderness, they saw abundant signs of life. NOTE [a] Some conditional clauses express an INDIRECT CONDITION, in that the condition
[b] The archaic forms whence [‘from where’] and whither [‘to where’] are is not related to the situation in the matrix clause. Here are some examples:
occasionally found, particularly in religious language.
His style is florid, if that’s the right word. (1)
If you remember your history lessons, the war was started by the
Clauses of condition, concession, and contrast
other side. [2]
15.17 There is considerable overlap in adverbial clauses that express condition, Lf you're going my way, | need a lift. (3]
concession, and contrast (¢f 15.15 Note [f]). She’s far too considerate, i 7 may say so.
The overlap between the three roles is highlighted by the overlapping
use of subordinators: for example, if introduces all three types of clauses
and whereas both contrast and concessive clauses. Furthermore, even if
expresses both the contingent dependence of one situation upon another
and the unexpected nature of this dependence:
Even if they offered to pay, 1 wouldn’t accept any money from them.
All three types of clauses tend to assume initial position in the
superordinate clause.
Conditional clauses
15.18 In general, conditional clauses convey a DIRECT CONDITION in that the
15.19 situation in the matrix clause is directly contingent on the situation in the
conditional clause. For example, in uttering [1] the speaker intends the
hearer to understand that the truth of the prediction ‘she’ll scream’
depends on the fulfilment of the condition of ‘your putting the baby
down’:
Ef you put the baby down, she'll scream. [1]
The most common subordinators for conditional clauses are if and
unless, which are also used with nonfinite and verbless clauses. Other
conditional subordinators are restricted to finite clauses (but cf Note [b]
below); for example: given (that) (formal), on condition (that), provided
(that), providing (that), supposing (that). Here are other examples of
conditional clauses:
Unless the strike has been called off, there will be no trains tomorrow.
He doesn’t mind inconveniencing others just so he’s comfortable.
<informal>
s
In uttering [4], the speaker does not intend the truth of the assertion 'She’s far too
considerate’ to be dependent on obtaining permission from the hearer. Rather, the
condition is dependent on the implicit speech act of the utterance: ‘I’m telling you,
ifI may, that she’s far too considerate.’ In conventional politeness, the speaker is
making the utterance of the assertion dependent on obtaining permission from the
hearer, though the fulfilment of that condition is conventionally taken for granted.
[b] Nonfinite and verbless clauses with with or without as subordinator may express
a conditional relationship:
Without me to supplement your income, you wouldn't be able to manage.
With them on our side, we are secure.
Open and hypothetical condition
A direct condition may be either an OPEX CONDITION or a HYPOTHETICAL
CONDITION. Open conditions are neutral: they leave unresolved the
question of the fulfilment or nonfulfilment of the condition, and hence
also the truth of the proposition expressed by the matrix clause:
Lf Colin is in London, he is undoubtedly staying at the Hilton.
The sentence leaves unresolved whether Colin is in London, and hence it
leaves unresolved whether he is staying at the Hilton.
A hypothetical condition, on the other hand, conveys the speaker’s
belief that the condition will not be fulfilled (for future conditions), is not
fulfilled (for present conditions). or was not fulfilled (for past conditions)
and hence the probable or certain falsity of the proposition expressed by
the matrix clause:
If he changed his options, he’d be a more likeable person. [1]
They would be here with us if they had the time. [2]
If you had listened to me, you wouldn't have made so many
mistakes, [3]

Adverbial clauses  319

style, particularly in argumentation.
He very probably won't change his opinions.

if the subordinator is though and must bei f it is as:

That is also used concessively with obligatory fronting ofa subject complement,
15.23 The universal conditional—concessive clause indicates a free choice from
normally a noun phrase; any number of conditions. It is introduced by the wh-words that combine
with -ever:
Fool that he was, he managed to evade his pursuers.
Poor that they were, they gave money to charity, <BrE) Whatever I say to them, I can't keep them quiet. [1]
Stand perfectly still, wherever you are.
Cf15.26 Note [b] for a similar fronting with reason clauses.
However much advice yau give him, he does exactly what he wants.
[b] Even if combines the concessive force of even with the conditional force of if:
Don’t let them in, whoever they are.
Even if you dislike ancient monuments, Warwick Castle is worth a visit,
The concessive implication in [1] comes through the inference that I can’t
The even if clause leaves open whether or not ‘you dislike ancient monuments’ is keep them quiet even if I choose to say something to them from any
true, whereas an even though clause would presuppose that it was true. possible choices.
Ifitself may be used concessively, synonymous either with even if or with even
though:
_ NOTE The verb be can be omitted from a universal clause if the subject of an SVC clause
It’s possible, if difficult. [‘. .., even if it may be difficult.’] is an abstract noun phrase:
Her salary was good, ifnot up to her expectations. [‘.. ., even though it was Whatever your problems (are/may be), they can’t be worse than mine.
not up to her expectations.’]
However great the pitfalls (are/may be), we must do our best to succeed.
Alternative conditional~concessive clauses
15,22 The correlative sequence whether . . . or (whether) combines the Clauses of contrast
conditional meaning of if with the disjunctive meaning of either . . . or. If 15.24 Clauses of contrast are introduced by several of the subordinators that
the second unit is a full finite clause, whether may be repeated: introduce concessive clauses (ef 15.21): whereas, while, and <esp BrE>
whilst. Indeed, there is often a mixture of contrast and concession. The
Whether Martin pays for the broken vase or (whether) he replaces it contrastive meaning may be emphasized by correlative antithetic con-
with a new vase, I'm not inviting Him again.
juncts such as in contrast and by contrast when the contrastive clause is
He’s getting married. whether or not he finds a job. initial:

putative rather than factual, purpose clauses require a modal auxiliary.

NOTE The subordinator so
is  indistinguishable  from  the conjuncsot  in  asyndetic «formal, or by the fronted correlative the .. . the followed by compara-
coordination, but if and is inserted so is unambiguously the conjunct:
tive forms:
We paid him immediately, and so he left contented.
As he grew disheartened, (so) his work deteriorated.
As the lane got narrower, (so) the overhanging branches made it
Clauses of similarity and comparison more difficult for us to keep sight of our quarry.

The subordinators as, as if, and as though can introduce nonfinite and
| verbless clauses: |     |     |     |     |     | Comment clauses |     |     |     |     |     |

15.32 Comment clauses are parenthetical disjuncts. They may occur initially,
Fill in the application form as instructed.
finally, or medially, and thus generally have a separate tone unit:
You should discuss the company with him as though unaware that
you were being considered for a job.

NOTE
Type (i) comment clauses, which are the most important, generally contain a
transitive verb or an adjective which elsewhere requires a nominal thai-clause as The subject of nonfinite and verbless clauses
complementation. We can therefore see a correspondence between sentences
containing such clauses and sentences containing indirect statements: 15.34 Nonfinite and verbless adverbial clauses that have an overt subject but are
There were no other applicants, / believe, for that job. not introduced by a subordinator are ABSOLUTE Clauses, so termed because
I believe that there were no other applicants for that job. they are not explicitly bound to the matrix clause syntactically. Absolute
clauses may be -ing, -ed, or verbless clauses:
Since the thar of an object rhat-clause is normally deletable, only the intonation
(reflected by comma separation in writing) distinguishes an initial comment clause No further discussion arising, the meeting was brought to a
close.
from an initial matrix clause: Lunch finished, the guests retired to the lounge,

Sometimes the attachment rule is violated:
They stood silently, their eyes fixed on the horizon.
?Driving to Chicago that night, a sudden thought struck me. [‘] Was Elizabeth dived in head first.
driving’)  They strolled through the park with their arms intertwined.
:

clauses are totally unacceptable if the sentence provides no means fo;
identifying the implied subject:

330 Syntactic and semantic functions of subordinate clauses
Comparative clauses 331
Clause functions of the comp-element
Morton has more expensive clothes than I have.
15.37 The comp-element ofa comparative construction can be any of the clause
elements, apart from the verb: In onc interpretation more modifies expensive (‘clothes that are more expensive’):
subject: in the other interpretation more is the determiner for the noun phrase (‘a greater
quantity of expensive clothes’).
More people use this brand than (use) any other window-cleaning [c] The modifying sequences more ofa... and less ofa... occur with gradable
fluid. singular noun heads:
direct object: He’s more ofa fool than I thought (he was).
It was less ofa success than I imagined {it would be).
She knows more history than most people (know).
Cfthe how-question, How much of a fool is he? [To what extent is he (in your view)
indirect object: a fool?’}
That toy has given more children happiness than any other (toy) There are parallel constructions with as much ofa...asand as little ofa... as:
(has). It was as much/little ofa success as | imagined (it would be).
subject complement: [d] When the contrast involves two points on the same scale, one higher than the
other, the part following than cannot be expanded into a clause, Than is then
Lionel is more relaxed than he used to be. functioning as a preposition in a nonclausal comparison;
object complement: It’s hotter ¢han just warm. (cf It's hotter than 90°.)
She's wiser shan merely clever.
She thinks her children more obedient than (they were) last year. We drove farther than (beyond) Chicago.
adverbial: They fought harder than that.
You’ve been working much harder than I (have). Another type of nonclausal comparison is expressed by more than:
The comp-element may also be a prepositional complement:

The same range is available for the comp-element in comparisons of on the inadequacy of what is said in the linguistic unit it modifies.
equivalence; for example: fe] Some people prefer to use so... as instead of as... as when the matrix clause is

As many people use this brand as (use) any other window-cleaning
fluid.
He’s not so naughty as he was.
NOTE [a] There is a type of nonclausal comparison in which more... than, less... than, Ellipsis in comparative clauses
and as... as are followed by an explicit standard of comparison:
15.38 _ Ellipsis ofa part of the comparative clause is likely to occur when that part
I weigh more than 200 pounds. is a repetition of something in the matrix clause. Since it is normal for the

set of examples of optional ellipsis and optional substitutions by
Another nonclausal comparison is exemplified by: pronouns and by pro-predicate or pro-predication:
T'was more angry than frightened. [‘It is more true to say that I was... .°] James and Susan often go to plays but
I was angry more than frightened.
(i) James enjoys the theatre more than Susan enjoys the theatre.
The inflectional form is not possible: (ii) James enjoys the theatre more than Susan enjoys it.
*] was angrier than frightened. (iii) James enjoys the theatre more than Susan does.
(iv) James enjoys the theatre more than Susan.
[b] When more precedes an adjective in a noun phrase, there may be ambiguity.
(v) James enjoys the theatre more.
For example, more expensive clothes is ambiguous in:
Ellipsis of the object generally cannot take place unless the main verb too

332 Syntactic and semantic functions of subordinate clauses
Comparative clauses 333
is ellipted, as in (iii) and (iv), where there is a choice between the retention
She'll enjoy it more than (she enjoved it) last year.
of an operator and its omission:
This provides the basis for the total ellipsis of the subordinate clause in
*James knows more about the theatre than Susan knows.
examples like:
The comp-element is the hinge between the matrix clause and the
You are slimmer (than you were).
comparative clause. Since the comp-element specifies the standard of
You're looking better (than you were (looking)).
comparison, the same standard cannot be specified again in the
comparative clause: NOTE [a] There are two other contexts in which the comparative clause is omitted. Onc is
*Jane is healthier than her sister is healthy. where there is anaphoric reference to an implied or actual preceding clause or
*James enjoys the theatre more than Susan much enjoys the theatre.
sentence:
The standards of comparison in the two clauses may, however, be Tcaught the bus from town: but Harry came home even later. (ie ‘later than I
different. came home’] :
The other is where the reference is to the extralinguistic situation:
Mary is cleverer than Jane is pretty.
You should have come home earlier, [iz “earlier than you did’]
Ambiguity through ellipsis
15.39 When normal ellipsis is taken to its fullest extent, ambiguity can arise as to [b] The partial contrast may be in a superordinate clause in the matrix clause or the
comparative clause:
whether a remaining noun phrase is subject or object:
She thinks she’s fatter than she (really) is.
He loves his dog more than his children. He's a greater painter than people suppose (he is).
The above example could mean either [1] ‘. .. than his children love his She enjoyed it more than/ expected (her to (enjoy it)).
dog’ or [2] ‘than he loves his children’. If his children is replaced by a
Enough and too
pronoun, formal English makes the distinction:
15.41 There are comparative constructions that express the contrasting notions
He loves his dog more than they. {] of sufficiency and excess, chiefly with enough and too followed by a to-
He loves his dog more than them. [2] infinitive clause. Paraphrase pairs may be constructed with antonymous
items:
In other styles, however, the objective case them is used for both [1] and [2}.
Since both forms can be criticized (on account of stiffness on the one hand, {ree rich enough to own a car.
and ‘bad grammar’ on the other), and since in any event we cannot be sure They’re not too poor to own a car.
that the objective case in [2] represents choice in formal style and is
therefore unambiguous, it is better to expand the clause (than they do; than The book is simple enough to understand.
he does them) where there is danger of ambiguity. The book is not too difficult to understand.
If the context allows, the infinitive clause may be omitted.
Partial contrasts The negative force of too is shown in the use of nonassertive forms.
Contrast:
15.40 If the two clauses in a comparison differed solely in the comp-element (*/
hear it more clearly than I hear it), the comparison would of course be She’s old enough to do some work.
nonsensical; therefore, a contrast of at least one variable is required She’s too old to do any work.
between the two clauses. The contrast may affect only tense or the
addition ofa modal auxiliary. In such cases it is normal to omit the rest of The infinitive clause may contain an overt subject:
the comparative clause after the auxiliary: It moves too quickly for most people to see (it).
I hear it more clearly than J did. [‘than I used to hear it’] He was old enough for us to talk to “him ) seriously.
I get up later than I should. [‘than I should get up’] As these examples indicate, a direct object or the object of a prepositional
If the contrast lies only in tense, it may be expressed in the comparative verb may be omitted if it substitutes for the superordinate subject.
clause solely by an adverbial: When there is no subject in the infinitive clause, it is identified with the
superordinate subject or with an indefinite subject:

334 Syntactic and semantic functions of subordinate clauses Comparative clauses 335
She writes quickly enough to finish the paper on time. [‘for her to There was such a crowd that we couldn't see a thing. [‘There was such
finish the paper on time’} a large crowd .. .”]
He was old enough fo talk to him seriously. [for others to talk to him
seriously] NOTE [a] The subordinator that may be omitted from the that-clause. An informal
variant substitutes intensifier that for so and omits the subordinator ¢har:
And there may be ambiguity as to which identification to make:
I was ‘that tired T couldn't keep my eyes open.
She was too young to date. ['... to date others’ or ‘for others to date
her’] ; [b] The somewhat formal construction so/such . . . as with the infinitive is
sometimes used in place of so/such with a that-clause:
When neither subject nor object is expressed in the infinitive clause,
ambiguity is possible with verbs that may be used transitively: His temper was so violent as to make even his closest companions fear him.
The brilliance of her satires was such as to make even her victims laugh.

Bibliographical note
On nominal clauses in general, see Huddleston (1971, Chs. 4 and 5); Stockwell et
al, (1973, Ch. 8); Vendler (1968). On that-clauses in particular, see Hooper (1975),
NOTE Enough ofa...and too much ofa... may be constructed with gradable nouns (¢f On adverbial clauses of time in general, see Edgren (1971),
15.37 Note [c]): On adverbial clauses of concession, see Aarts (1988): Altenberg (1986).
On clauses of reason, see Altenberg (1984).
He’s {enough fora coward to do that.
too much On comment clauses, see Lakoff, G, (1974).
On nonfinite and verbless adverbial clauses introduced by a-subordinator, see
Cfalso: He was fool enough to go out without a coat. Backlund (1984).
On adverbial -ing clauses, see Greenbaum (1973).
So... (that) and such... (that) On comparative clauses, see especially Huddleston (1971, Ch. 6).
15.42 The correlatives so... (that) and such... (that) introduce constructions
that combine the notion of sufficiency or excess with that of result. So is an
adverb premodifying an adjective or adverb, and such is a predeterminer.
Paraphrase pairs may be obtained between these constructions when the
that-clause is negative and constructions with too and an infinitive clause:
It’s so good a movie that we mustn’t miss it.
~It’s too good a movie fo miss.
It was such a pleasant day that I didn’t want to go to school.
~ It was roo pleasant a day to go to school.
There may be also similar paraphrases with constructions with enough
when the that-clause is positive:
It flies so fast that it can beat the speed record.
~It flies fast enough to beat the speed record.
T had such a bad headache thar I needed two aspirins.
~TJ had a bad enough headache ro need two aspirins.
When so is used alone with a verb and such is used with a noun that is
not premodified, they express a high degree and the construction conveys
the notion of result:
I so enjoyed it thar I’m determined to go again. [I so much enjoyed
it... .7]

The particle tends to precede the object if the object is long, or if it jg
intended that the object should receive end-focus (cf 18.2, 18.5). On the other hand, we can easily insert an adverbial between the lexical
Like intransitive phrasal verbs, transitive phrasal verbs are dis. verb and the preposition:
tinguished semantically from free combinations of verb and adverb, Many people /ooked disdainfully az the picture.

with the free combination in She took in the box [‘brought inside’], where By contrast, insertion between verb and direct object is usually avoided
the two parts preserve their separate meanings. unless the direct object is long:
If the transitive phrasal verb is fully idiomatic, the particle cannot 7*Many people examined disdainfully the picture.
normally be separated from the lexical verb by anything except the object,

not even by an intensifier such as right. Hence, bring up is a free
combination in She brought the girls right up [‘led them up’ (the stairs, ways, eg:
etc)], since the phrasal verb bring up [‘rear’] does not allow the On whom did he call? On his mother.
interruption. He called on his mother and on his sister.
He called on his mother more often than on his sister.

16.5 A type I prepositional verb consists of a lexical verb followed by a
preposition with which it is semantically and/or syntactically associated: Here are some examples of the prepositional passive:
Look at these pictures. Though something very different from ordinary forest management
I don’t care for Jane’s parties. is called for, the trees in the parks do need the forester’s skilled
We must go into the problem. consideration.
Can you cope with the work? This matter will have to be dealt with immediately.
I approve of their action. Other possibilities are talked of by manv of our colleagues.
His eyes lighted upon the jewel. Ifa woman witha university degree rejects a career for marriage, her
education is not to be thought of as thrown away unless we count
The noun phrase following the preposition is a PREPOSITIONAL OBJECT, a
the family arena of no importance.
term that suggests an analogy with the term direct object. Compare the
‘transitive’ relationship of /ook at and these pictures in Look at these A second criterion is that w/-questions eliciting the prepositional object

pictures with that of examine and these pictures in Examine these pictures.

John called on her.~ Who(im) did John call on?

The distinction between prepositional verbs and phrasal verbs Jenny thanked us for the present,
May I remind you of our agreement?
16.7 Type I prepositional verbs resemble transitive phrasal verbs superficially,
They have provided the child with a good education.
but the differences are both syntactic and phonological. The contrast is
exemplified for the prepositional verb cal/ on (‘visit’) and the phrasal verb The direct object becomes the subject in the corresponding passive clause:
call up (‘summon’). The gang robbed her of her necklace.
(a) The particle of a prepositional verb must precede the prepositional
~ She was robbed of her necklace (by the gang).
object (unless the particle is stranded), but the particle of a phrasal

Only the active direct object can be made passive subject with these:
Intransitive verbs
Our success can be put down to hard work.
16.11 Where no complementation occurs, the verb has an INTRANSITIVE use.
For both types, the wh-question eliciting the prepositional object is Some verbs are always intransitive:
formed with the pronouns whofm1) and what (¢f 16.6):
John has arrived. Your views do not matter.
She looked in on Mrs Johnson on our way back.
Others can also be transitive with the same meaning and without a
~(Who(m)) did she look in on?
change in the subject~verb relationship:
They put their success down to hard work.
~ What did they put their success down to? He smokes (a pipe). She is reading (a book).
In some cases the intransitive verb acquires a more specific meaning: eg:
Other multi-word verb constructions
John drinks (heavily) (‘drinks alcohol’).
16.10 In addition to the types of multi-word verbs discussed in 16.3-9, some Other intransitive verbs can also be transitive, but the semantic
other idiomatic verb constructions may be noted: connection between subject and verb is different:
(a) VERB—ADJECTIVE COMBINATIONS
The car stopped. She stopped the car.
These are similar to phrasal verbs. Compare:
Meg put the cloth straight. Meg put the cat out. NOTE Intransitive verbs include intransitive phrasal verbs. eg: fall out [‘quarrel’]. cf 16.3.
The constructions may be copular, eg: break even, plead guilty, lie low. Or
Copular verbs
they may be complex-transitive with a direct object following the verb (or
the adjective if the object is long) eg: cur (their trip) short, work (the nail) Subject complement

There is also a tendency with such copulas, especially in informal AmE, to prefer a do not allow the passive. These MIDDLE veRBS include have, fit, suit,
construction in which the verb is followed by like: It seems like the only solution, resemble, equal, mean (‘Oculist’ means ‘eye doctor’), contain, hold (The
{b] Some copulas are severely restricted as to the words that may occur in their hall holds over three hundred people), comprise, lack.
complement. The restriction may be to certain adjectives or to a sernantic set of
words. Here are some examples, with typical adjective complements: loom (large), They have a large house. ~*A large house is had (by them).
fall (silent), plead (innocent), rest (assured), run (wild), spring (open). See also
10.7 Note [b]. NOTE There are also monotransitive phrasal verbs, eg: bring about, put off (cf 16.4),
These take a direct object and can be used in the passive.
Complementation by adverbials _ Noun phrase as prepositional object
16.13 The principal copula that allows an adverbial as complementation is be. 16.16 The prepositional object of type I prepositional verbs (cf 16.5) and type I
The adverbials are mainly space adjuncts (eg: The kitchen is downstairs) phrasal—prepositional verbs (cf 16.9) resembles the direct object in
but time adjuncts are common with an eventive subject (eg: The party will accepting the passive (though often with some awkwardness in style) and
be at nine) and other types of adjunct are possible too (eg: She is in good in being elicited by a pronoun in questions:
health). Two other copula verbs that occur with space adjuncts (or
The management paid for his air fares.
adjuncts metaphorically related to these) are get (eg: How did you get
here?) and keep (eg: They kept out of trouble).
~ His air fares were paid for by the management.
~~ What did the management pay for?
With intransitive verbs such as live, come, go, lie, remain, stand, and
Your sister has checked up on me.
stay, the adverbial is not always clearly obligatory. But the positional or
~ Ihave been checked up on by vour sister.
directional meaning of these verbs is completed by the adverbials:
~ Who(m) has your sister checked up on?
My aunt lives i Toronto.
They are staying nearby. NOTE Another indication of the closeness of a prepositional object to a direct object is
Come over here. ; that when a prepositional verb is followed by a rhat-clause or a to-infinitive clause.
a the preposition disappears and the prepositional object merges with the direct
NOTE [a] The verbs seem, appear, look, sound, feel, smell, and taste may be complemented object of the monotransitive pattern:
by an adverbial clause beginning as if or as though: It seems as if the weather is on the meeting.
improving.
They agreed 4 (that) they would meet.
[b] Behave is complemented by a manner adverbial (He behaved badly) and last and to meet each other.
take by a duration adverbial (The course lasted (for) three months).
Yet the preposition that is omitted before a that-clause can reappear in the
Monotransitive verbs corresponding passive: That they should meet was agreed (on). This is so even in
extraposition (¢f 18.23), where the preposition immediately follows the passive
16.14 Monotransitive verbs require a direct object, which may bea noun phrase, verb phrase:
a finite clause, or a nonfinite clause. We include in this category, for our
It was agreed (on) that they should meet.

HYPOTHESIS verbs comprise wish, suppose (in the imperative), and the
We distinguish four categories of verbs that are complemented by that- modal idiom would rather or its contraction ‘d rather. They are followed
clauses: FACTUAL, SUASIVE, EMOTIVE, and HYPOTHESIS. Most verbs belong by a that-clause with the hypothetical past or the were-subjunctive.
to the first two categories. The four categories are distinguished
semantically, but also by the types of verbs that appear in the that-clauses, I wish (that) she {augi es
Factual verbs
16.18 FACTUAL verbs are followed by a thar-clause with an indicative verb:
Complementation by an extraposed subject rhat-clause
They agreed that she ways misled. 16,21 The that-clause in examples like It seems (that) you are mistaken is an
There are two subtypes of factual verbs. PUBLIC verbs consist of speech act extraposed subject, not an object of the verb. It resembles other thar-
verbs introducing indirect statements; PRIVATE verbs express intellectual clauses in previous sections in that the conjunction is optional and the
states and intellectual acts that are not observable. clause is obligatory. The verb in the rhat-clause is indicative:
Examples of public factual verbs: admit, agree, announce, argue, bet, It appears (that) you have lost your temper.
claim, complain, confess, declare, deny, explain, guarantee, insist, mention,
Common verbs in this pattern include seem, appear, and happen, and the
object, predict, promise, reply, report, say, State, suggest, swear, warn,
write.
phrasal verbs come about [‘happen’] and turn out [‘transpire’].
Examples of private factual verbs: believe, consider, decide, doubt,
Wh-clause as object
expect, fear,f eel, forget, guess, hear, hope, know, notice, presume, realize,
recognize, remember, see, suppose, think, understand. 16.22 Many of the factual verbs which can take a that-clause as object can also
take a wh-interrogative clause (¢f 15.4).
Suasive verbs
16.19 SUASIVE verbs are followed by a thar-clause either with putative should I don’t know if we can get there in time.
(preferred in BrE) or with the subjunctive (cf 14.13/). A third possibility. a Have you /eard whether she’s coming with us?
that-clause with an indicative verb, occurs, though more commonly in I doubt whether the flight has been booked.
BrE: The use of the wh-interrogative clause (which generally implies lack of
should leave knowledge on the part of the speaker) is particularly common where the
People are demanding that he {rere \ the company. superordinate clause is interrogative or negative. But verbs that them-
leaves <esp BrE> selves express uncertainty, such as ask and doubt, occur without this
nonassertive constraint.

depend, doubt, explain, forget, hear, know. mind, notice, prove, realize,

The secretary left all the letters unopened. [}] Space position adjuncts also occur in this construction:
I have often wished myself a millionaire. [2]
Always keep your eyes on the road when driving.
The long walk made us all /uangry. [3]
The attackers caught us off our guard.
The committee has elected you its chairman. [4]
The passive of this construction is exemplified for this last sentence:
The direct object can be made the passive subject:
We were caught off our guard (by the attackers).
All the letters were left unopened (by the secretary). [ta]
Adjuncts of other semantic types are less common, but they include a
Many verbs admit both adjective phrases and noun phrases as object
manner adjunct with treat (Her parents treated her badly).
complements. The most common verbs for this construction are listed
below. Those used only with adjective phrases are followed by ‘[A]’, and Direct object and to-infinitive clause
those used only with noun phrases are followed by ‘[NJ’: 16.27 Some of the verbs taking a direct object and to-infinitive clause in
appoint [N], believe, call, choose [N], consider, declare, elect [N}, find, complex-transitive complementation correspond to the factual verbs that

get [A], like [A], make, name [N], prefer [A], think, want [A].
The police reported the traffic to be heavy. (formal)
NOTE [a] For some verbs, the object complement is optional; eg: elect in The committee = The police reported that the traffic was heavy.
has elected you (its chairman). John believed the stranger to be'a policeman. <formal)
[b] Prepositional verbs, mainly with the preposition as, take a prepositional object
= John believed that the stranger was a policeman.
complement; eg: They described her as a genius; He took me for a fool. Sometimes
the preposition is optional; eg: They elected me (as) their leader. Common In such cases, the infinitive clause normally contains a verb used statively,
examples of these prepositional verbs follow, with those taking an optional especially be. The finite clause is preferred in normal usage, but the
preposition listed first: choose (as), consider (as), elect (as) [N), make (into) [N}; infinitive clause provides a convenient passive form:
accept as, define as, intend as {N], mistake for, regard as, see as, take as/for, treat as,
use as. The traffic was reported to be heavy.
[c] The SVOC pattern includes a number of verb-adjective collocations; for
Common factual verbs: believe, consider, expect, feel, find, know, suppose.
example: boil (an egg) hard, buy [N] cheap, freeze [N] hard, paint [N] red/blue . . .,
Nonfactual verbs include verbs ofi ntention, causation, modality, and
knock (someone) senseless, The adjectives open, loose, free, and clean are
particularly common: push [N] open, shake [N] loose, set [N] free, wipe [N] clean. purpose:
[d] The object is generally postposed by extraposition if it is a that-clause, and an They intended Maria to sing an aria.
anticipatory it then precedes the object complement:
The meeting elected her to be the next treasurer.
I think it very odd that nobody is in. My contract allows me to take one month’s leave.
Our teachers encouraged us to think for ourselves.
The collocations make sure and make certain are followed by an object that-clause
without anticipatory ir: Common nonfactual verbs: allow, appoint. cause, compel, condemn, dare,
Please make sure that you enclose your birth certificate. get, help, intend, mean, permit, require.

Secondly, with a personal antecedent. the relative pronoun can show
the relative pronoun. It is capable (a) of showing agreement with the head,
the distinction between who and whom, depending
and  (b)  of indicating its  status  as  an  element  in  the relative clause on its role as subject of
the relative clause or as object or as prepositional complement:
structure.

Nonrestrictive postmodification
VAT Postmodification with nonfinite clauses can also be nonrestrictive: Postmodification by prepositional phrases
The apple tree, swaying gently in the breeze, had a good crop of fruit.
(‘which was swaying . . .’)
Relation to more explicit modifiers
The substance, discovered almost by accident, has revolutionized 17.19 A prepositional phrase is by far the commonest type of postmodification
medicine. (‘which was discovered . . .”) in English: it is three or four times more frequent than either finite or
This scholar, to be seen daily in the British Museum, has devoted his nonfinite clausal postmodification. The full range of prepositions is
life to the history of science. (‘who can be seen. . .”) involved:
These clauses can be moved to initial position without change of meaning, The road to Lincoln Two years before the war
but in that case they can no longer be expanded into finite relative clauses. A tree by a stream Aman from the electricity board
Indeed, they have an implicit semanti¢ range beyond that of a relative The house beyond the church This book on grammar
clause (cf 15.35). Thus the nonfinite clause in this example:
including the complex prepositions (9.3):
The woman, wearing such dark glasses, obviously could not see
Action in case offi re Passengers on board the ship
clearly.
could be a reduction of a relative clause ‘who was wearing...’ or ofa and including those having participial form:
causal clause ‘because she was wearing...’ or of a temporal clause such as A delay pending further inquiry
‘whenever she wore...’.
Among the prepositions less commonly used in postmodification we

there’. But it is common and fully acceptable in the sense ‘such as’:
Appositive postmodification A man like John would never do that.

One of the chief reasons for preferring the of-phrase to the -s genitive is to
avoid discontinuity (with unwanted humour); thus:
Multiple modification
The ears of the man in the deckchair

.  On the other hand, the group genitive construction enables us to postpose

regarded as ‘found’ after it has been retrieved, a purse will be regarded as

With multiple head
| with  the  | conventionalizing  | of a  | sequence  |                        |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------- | ------------------ | ----- | --------- | ---------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|            |                    |       |           | in  the  direction  of |     |     |     |     |     |     |
compounding.  17.37 Modification may apply to more than one head (ef 13.25):
.
| A  notable  | constraint  against  | making  | postmodifying  | phrases  into | The new table |     |     |     |     |     |
| ----------- | -------------------- | ------- | -------------- | ------------- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
The new chairs } ~ The new table and chairs
premodifying nouns is the relative impermanence of the modification in
question. Thus while The table in the corner will readily yield The corner
table, we cannot do the same with The multiple head thus produced can now be subject to recursive or
coordinate modification:
The girl in the corner (spoke to me)~*The corner girl...
|     |     |     |     |     | T   |     | .   | T h e   b e au | t if |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | -------------- | ---- | --- |
We must insist again that this is nota property of the lexical item (in this he new table and cha irs {t u l   n e w  t a b le  a n d  c h a ir s
|     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     | h e   n e w |  ( b u t )   u g ly   ta | b le  a n d  c h a i rs |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ----------- | ------------------------ | ----------------------- |
instance, corner) but of the semantic relation; cf 17.4.
If we coordinated learned Papers and books as in (He wrote ) learned
papers and books, we would suggest that learned applies to both papers and
books. If it should not, we can either reorder (b00ks and learned papers) or
Multiple premodification
introduce separate determiners (some learned papers and some books).
With single head
With modified modifier
| 17.36 The three types  | of multiple modification specified  |     |     |                           |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------- | --- | --- | ------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|                        |                                     |     |     | in  17.25  apply to 17.38 |     |     |     |     |     |     |
premodification also. More than one premodifier may be related to a We have already seen two types of modification with modified modifier:
single head, with no grammatical limit on the number:
His really quite unbelievably delightful cottage (17.29)
These French women's clothing (17.34)
His brilliant boo~ Hkis  last boo~ Hkis  (. . .) book.
~ His last brilliant (. . .) book
In a third type, the noun premodifier can be itself premodified by cither
|     |     |     |     |     | adjective  | or a  noun  and,  | if the  | latter. this  |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------- | ----------------- | ------- | ------------- | --- | --- |
This is, however, misleading in giving the impression that the multiple can  in  turn  be  similarly
premodified:
| modifiers  constitute  | an  unordered  | and  | coordinate  | string.  |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------------------- | -------------- | ---- | ----------- | -------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
It  usually
follows a recursive process:
|     |     |     |     |     | The office furniture —  |     | The small office furniture |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ----------------------- | --- | -------------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
The tax office furniture
| His book—His brilliant  |     | book  His  | [last (brilliant book)] |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ----------------------- | --- | ---------- | ----------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
cme Property tax office furnituree——_|
We would here mean that, of several brilliant books, we are speaking only
of his last one; by contrast The house property tax office furniture
It should be noted, however, that if we were to introduce an adjective in
His book—His last book His [brilliant (last book)]
this last noun phrase, already clumsy and improbable, (i) it would have to

You'll meet a@ man tomorrow carrying a heavy parcel.
There are more striking examples:
Thad a nice glass of beer but in an ugly glass.
This is not as contradictory as it may seem, since it is only in the second
noun phrase that glass is premodified by an adjective; in the first, it is
better to regard glass of beer as a complex unit modified as a whole but
with glass being less a concrete noun than a unit of measure. So too witha
|     | weak cup  | of tea, and phrases of the form kind/sort of N which take |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

concord:

are uttered with sequentially increasing prominence, with the S conveying
least information, the V rather more, and the A conveying most, namely,
the information sought by the wh-element of the question ‘When (shall we
know what Mary is going to do)?’ A TONE UNIT (2.15) is a stretch of speech
Introduction containing one intonation nucleus, and since each such nucleus serves to
highlight a piece of information, it follows that a tone unit is coextensive
18.1 In the processing and receiving of information, whether written or with an INFORMATION UNIT.
spoken, lexical choice and grammatical organization have an important But although an information unit highlights one item, this does not
role. Consider these examples: mean that the rest of the unit is devoid of information. As in the present
Will the new law help old people? [i] example, the ‘communicative dynamism’ can range from very low
The road will ultimately be repaired. [2] (corresponding to weak stress, as with the subject she), through medium
T'll visit them occasionally. [3] (corresponding to nonnuclear stress, as with the verb phrase, will decide),
The honeymoon couple returned to Edinburgh in bright to very strong stress (corresponding to intonation nucleus, as with the
sunshine today. [4] adverbial, next week). And, again as in this example, it is common —
though by no means necessary — for the range of such communicative
In the following variants, the truth value is fundamentally unchanged, but dynamism to increase from low to high in accordance with the linear
the presentation is very different: progression of the information unit. To put it another (and better) way, it
In your view, will the new law give old people the help they is common to process the information in a message so as to achieve a
need? [la] linear presentation from low to high information value. We shall refer to
this as the principle of END-Focus.

Emphasis may be given to an initial noun phrase (or indeed to any Consider an exchange like the following:
norfinal item) by interposing a parenthesis with its own tone unit:
A: So what did you sAy?
B: There was nothing TO say.
| This in sHORT | is| why I rerWsED |

Similarly, within a noun phrase, if the head is more general and carries less
semantic weight and specificity than the premodifier, it is the latter that may ‘Was that how you became a  rustler?”
sometimes be focused: A rustler?’ I repeated. ‘Not a rustler, Miss Cronjager — a wrestler.’

NOTE Where marked focus is required on a preposition. it is sometimes possible to
I've been CHEATed by the dirty scoundrel. achieve the required meaning with end-focus by using a paraphrase. Thus beside:
-.. apart from his Laziness |

than where it would occur in unmarked focus. But there are two further
types, (a) and (b), to be considered: ... apART from his ‘laziness |
(a) First, the focus can be moved to a point subsequent or immediately
or:
prior to its expected position. This is sometimes because the unmarked
focus is misleading, as it might be in: ..- his ‘laziness apArt |
With the adverbial particle in phrasal verbs. the same result is achieved merely by
| Who’s the Novel by |
movement:

(3] suggests a context in which there is discussion of what I had done in 1980, this Allthough ‘John is rAzy |...
part therefore being relatively given; [4] suggests one in which the discussion (5]
| Lazy though John fs J...
concerns when I went to France, the rise again coinciding with the relatively given, [6]
but this time preceding the relatively new instead of following it. In [3], /azy is new information; there is no assumption on the speaker’s
part that the listener knows. In [4], the fall-rise on lazy also implies that
the information is new; the rise part of the complex nucleus is conditioned
by the dependent status of the clause of which this is the focus. In [5],
however, the simple rise on /azy is compatible both with the dependence of

speaker's view of John. In [6], making /asy a marked theme again implies
18.13 The two communicatively significant parts of an information unit, the
the givenness of the information and additionally enables the speaker to
theme and the focus, are typically as distinct as they can be: one is the
point of initiation, and the other the point of completion. The theme ofan focus upon the operator (cf 18.1 1) with consequent emphasis
on  its
Positive polarity (¢f 3.11). A further example: ‘Serious as has become the
information unit, coming first, is more often ‘given’ information than any
food shortage, worse news is to follow’; this embodies inversion of subject
other part of it. Yet the two can coincide; for instance, when, as marked
| focus, the nucleus falls on the subject of a statement: |     |     |     |     | and verb (ef 18.16). |     |     |     |     |

(Who gave you that magazine?)  | BiLL gave it to me  |  {1] NOTE Common short adverbials in initial position are often given some thematic
marking:

| Then he rrr |
has an unmarked theme (he) with minimum prosodic prominence:
He | gave me a magazine  |  [2] Longer and semantically weightier adverbials at J will be more heavily marked

| Clearly,        | theme  and  focus  | must  coincide  | in  one-word  utterances, |     |     |                      |                        |     |     |

| whether  these  | are  questions,    | responses,      | or  military  commands.   | For |     |                      |                        |     |     |
example: Note that marked theme can be used to draw attention to contrasting pairs, and
this often involves separate tone units:
| | corFee  | |  | THANKS  | |   |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |

Even so, many such short units have an initial portion that can be used as
|     |     |     |     |     |     | perFection  | |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- |
thematic preparation. A striking instance of this is found in the military
order, ‘Attention!’ The word is typically uttered with considerable drawl
on the first two syllables, and (ignoring the fact that the word ordinarily
has stress on the second syllable) with the final syllable given word stress
| and the climactic nuclear focus: |     |     |     |     | Fronting |     |     |     |     |

| at|ten—TiON                      | |   |     |     |     |          |     |     |     |     |

Fronting is the term we apply to the achievement of marked theme by

410 Theme, focus, and information processing
Cleft sentences 411
Here’s the milkman.
And there at last was the book I’d been looking for.
Least of all is it in our interest to open negotiations
now,
At no time must this door be left unlocked.
Down came the rain.
He refused to apologize. Nor would he offer any explanation

(c) Thirdly, we have Sop inversion in comparative clauses when the Sis

not a personal pronoun:
SVA order invites us not merely to put the nuclear focus upon the A but to
see these adjuncts as referring to specific places. Compare: Oil costs less than would atomic energy. (cf Oil costs less than it did).
She looks forward, as does her secretary, to the completion of the
Here’s the milkman — he’s come at last.
building.

(d) Finally, S~op inversion occurs in subordinate clauses of condition
There’s the book I want ~ I’ve been looking for it all week.
and concession (cf 15.19 Note [c]), especially in rather formal usage:
The book is there ~ by the typewriter.
Were we to withdraw our support, they would be justifiably
NOTE Subject—verb inversion (as distinct from subject-operator inversion; ¢f 18.17) with indignant.
fronted object chiefly represents direct speech (including speech that is ‘thought') Should you change your plans, please let me know.
and usually the subject is not a personal pronoun: Even had she left a will, it is unlikely that the college would have
benefited.
‘Please go away,’ said one child. ‘And don’t come back,’ pleaded another,
This is something of a literary convention, and in ordinary speech, VS would Ifan initial negative item is the vehicle for only a local negation (¢f 10.40), no S-op
usually be replaced by SV (ef 14.17). More important are CVS, AVS, where the C inversion is possible. Thus, with the sentence adjunct (¢f 8.15) in:
or A make comparative reference to something that has preceded:
Not without reason, Charles had flown into a rage.
His answer was a disgrace and equally regrettable was his departure = "He had flown into a rage and it was not without reason’
immediately afterwards.
Contrast, with predication adjunct (ef 8.14):
Subject—operator inversion Not without reason had Charles flown into a rage.
= ‘He hadn’t flown into a rage without reason’
18.17 In addition to the inversion in questions, there are four common
circumstances in which the operator precedes the subject.
(a) First, we have elliptical clauses with initial so or the corresponding
negatives neither or nor (cf 12.13):
John saw the accident and so did Mary. Cleft sentences
{cf ...and Mary did (so), too]
John didn’t see the accident and { neither hid Mary. 18.18 In 18.14, we looked at examples of where heightened prominence was
?nor achieved with no other grammatical change involved beyond fronting.
[cf... and Mary didn’t, either]
For example:
She was angry and so was I.
He won't go and neither should you. His callousness 1 shall ignore. [1]
But inversion is less common with certain modal auxiliaries (notably may, We now turn to devices for giving prominence by more elaborate
might, ought), and alternative substitute expressions with normal order grammatical means, involving the division of the sentence into two
are preferred: clauses, each with its own verb:
She might be ill and he might (be) too. It is his callousness that I shall ignore.
What I shall ignore is his callousness.
(b) Secondly, we have S-op inversion where a phrase of negative form
The thing I shall ignore is his callousness.
or meaning is fronted (cf 10.35/):
His callousness is something I shall ignore.

It is the dog that scared me. [b} Though the verb form in the first clause of a cleft sentence is usually simple
present or past, forms with modals are perfectly possible:
In the relative-clause version, the S could be replaced by another pronoun (such as
It may be his father that you're thinking of.
this) and that could be replaced by which. In cleft sentences, such alternatives are
not generally acceptable. It would have been at that time that she went to live near Mannheim.
18.19 The flexibility of the cleft-sentence device can be seen in the ease with Where the verb of the second clause is present. that of the first will be present:
which different parts can be highlighted. Consider the sentence:
It is novels that Miss Williams enjoys reading,
John wore a white suit at the dance last night. Where the second verb is past, the first can be past:
From this, four cleft sentences can be derived. In the following, we shall It was novels that Miss Williams enjoyed as a pastime.

15.7f). It thus differs from the ordinary cleft sentence in being completely
accountable in terms of the categories of main clause and subordinate
clause discussed in Chapter 14. The following are virtually synonymous:
It’s a good rest that you need most.
A good rest is what you need most.
Postponement
The pseudo-cleft sentence occurs more typically, however, with the wh-
clause as subject, since it can thus present a climax in the complement:
One important communicative difference between the two types of cleft
construction is that while the cleft sentence with it is often used to put the
What you need most is a good rest.
main focus near the front of the sentence, the pseudo-cleft is chiefly used to
Unlike the cleft sentence, it rather freely permits marked focus to fall on
postpone the focus to end position. In this respect it is often in competition
the predication: with  the  passive.  In  [1],  focus  is  placed  on  the  noun  phrase  the
manufacturers by means of the passive, and in [2] by means of a pseudo-
What he’s done is (to) spoil the whole thing.
cleft ‘paraphrase’:
Here we would expect an anticipatory (rising) focus on the do item, the
|     |     |     |     |     |     | The device was tested by the manufacturers.  |     |     |     |     | [1] |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
main focus coming at normal end-focus position. Thus: ‘... DONE...
|     |                 |          |            |                   |              | The people who tested the device were the manufacturers.  |     |     |     |     | [2] |
| --- | --------------- | -------- | ---------- | ----------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | When  the verb  | in  the  | wh-clause  | has  progressive  | aspect,  the |                                                           |     |     |     |     |     |
THinG’.
complement matches it with an -ing clause: It should be noted that [2] presupposes that the hearer knows that testing
has taken place; with [1] this is not so.
What I’m doing is teaching him Japanese.
Given the importance of end-focus (cf 18.2), it is not surprising that
But in some respects, the pseudo-cleft sentence is more limited than the
English has numerous resources to ensure the distribution of information
cleft sentence proper. It is indeed only with what-clauses that we can make according to our wishes. There are, for example, lexical and grammatical
a direct comparison (or choice) between the two constructions. Clauses devices which reverse the order of roles:
with where and when are sometimes acceptable, but mainly when the wi-
|     |     |     |     |     |     | An uncle, three cousins, and two brothers benefited from  |     |     |     |     | the will. |

clause is subject complement:
The will benefited an uncle, three cousins, and two brothers.
Here is where the accident took place.
(In) Autumn is when the countryside is most beautiful. An unidentified blue liquid was in the bottle.
The bottle contained an unidentified blue liquid.
Clauses introduced by who, whose, why, and how do not easily enter into
A red sports car was behind the bus.
the pseudo-cleft sentence construction at all, and to compensate for these

construction involving noun phrases of general reference in place of the

To settle the matter was my prime objective. we have three further possibilities. First, with ordinary it extraposition:
But: | made settling the matter my prime objective. It is difficult to pour cream out of this jug.
*I made it my prime objective settling the matter. The two other possibilities are:
I owe it to you that the jury acquitted me. This | jug is Dirficult to pour CREAM out of|
|Cream is pificult to pour out of this 1c |
Cf: *I owe that the jury acquitted me to you.
Contrast: 1 owe my acquittal to you. The former implies difficulties with the jug (perhaps its spout is too narrow); the
SVOA [with corresponding nominalization] latter implies difficulties with cream (perhaps it is too thick).
Something put it into his head that she was a spy.
Postponement of object in SVOC and SVOA clauses
Cf: *Something put that she was a spy into his head.
Something put the idea of her being a spy into his head, 18.26 When the object is a long and complex phrase, final placement for end-
focus or end-weight is possible in SVOC and SVOA clause types. This
does not involve an it-substitution.
The construction type She’s a pleasure to teach (a) Shift from SVO,C, order to SVC,O, order:

object or prepositional complement, the noun phrase concerned can He had called an idiot the man on whose judgment he now had to rely.
sometimes be fronted to become the theme in place of it. For example:

To teach Elizabeth is a pleasure.

days without food.

They made an embarrassing protest to the authorities. generally serviceable (though it tends to be rather informal in tone) is to
replace the intransitive verb by a transitive one of very general meaning
In such instances, revision is essential to make it clear which of the two possible
meanings is intended: and give it as eventive object a nominalization of the intransitive item (cf

The commonest prepositional phrase discontinuities are of the type:

Note also the interruption of a verb phrase by the insertion of adverbials at M; of
So also solve~find a solution;
8.11. In writing, it is often convenient to use an adverbial along with the emphatic agree~ reach (or come to) an agreement;
operator where prosodic prominence would have sufficed in speech: apply~ submit  an  application;  sugges~t  offer  (or  make)
a  suggestion;
permit ~ grant (or give) permission; attend~ pay attention, etc.
They did indeed find a solution. ~ They | FOUND a  solution |

It can act as subject in infinitive and -ing clauses:
(ii)  As with cleft sentences, too, we can have different tenses in the two parts
of the sentence. Compare:
I don’t want there to be any misunderstandings.
He was disappointed at there being so little to do.
Some planets were discovered by the ancients.

),  :
They're | all the Same, these politicians |
The tag can be inserted parenthetically, and need not be final:
He’s got a good future, your brother, if he perseveres.
NOTE {a] An even more informal type of tag comprises a subject and operator:
That was a
lark, that was!
He likes a drink now and then, Jim does.
She’s a good player, Ann (is).
In some dialects of English (especially Northern Br), the operator may precede
the subject:
She’s a good player, is Ann.
f
’ [b] Postposed nonfinite clauses, of the kind discussed in 18.24, sometimes closely
resemble amplificatory tags; contrast:
It was tough getting the job finished on time.
| {|tough...  | ... TiME]] |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ----------- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
It was tough, getting beaten in the last match.
| [|toucH]/...  | 0... mArcH]] |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------- | ------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
[c] Expletives (in the broadest sense) provide a common mode of amplification in
extremely informal speech, serving as a rhetorical transition between theme and an
emotionally coloured focus:
I| told them to ‘darned ‘well Listen |
Expletives can also amplify the theme in wh-questions: “How on earth did you lose
it?’

The partially antonymous home and abroad, ashore and on board are Since  time  passes  irrespective  of location  (which  need  not  change),
exceptional in combining the dimension and location factors: temporal cues to periods, and to references before, after, within, and during
these periods, are more inherently essential than locational cues,

19.36 Questions, direct and indirect, have an important role in discourse as question so common in the most informal speech is strictly similar to the
polite equivalents of requests. On entering someone’s room, a  rhetorical question in its communicative effect, since it essentially seeks
visitor will

declarative) to be agreed truth:

celebratory behaviour, we have decisions to make on similar principles to

| however, the order will probably be contextually determined:  |     |     |     | one or |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | ------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
other, the arrival or the seeing, will be relatively ‘given’ (that is, the hearer/
reader will already have been told or been led to expect that the subject
went home or that a window had been broken), and whichever is in
niaf
consequence the reiatively ‘new’ item will be placed in final position if a
feeble anticlimactic ‘tail’ is to be avoided. So again with [Sa] and [Sb]: the
sea gil former will be preferred if the preceding part of the text has been dealing
with ‘past crises’ and the intention is to go on to some discussion of the
 sva future; if the converse holds, [5b] will be selected.
 ad ece
Some matters of sequence are deterthined by courtesy, convention, or idiom. In
NOTE
formal circumstances, women are named in address before men, and the speaker is
mentioned last:
Ladies and gentlemen! It gives me great pleasure . . .
Harry and I were dismayed.
